title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles global biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover (A1); the 2026 multi‑country cruise‑ship outbreak of Andes virus illustrates the unpredictable, global threat of rodent‑borne orthohantaviruses (A2); a macro‑ecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards has been lacking (A3).
analysis.methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework combined 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection (FOI) modeling to project global spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060 (A4).
analysis.results: Models show a spatially uneven but extensive expansion, with ~74.9% of modeled land area experiencing increased FOI (A5); three high‑hazard belts are identified in western/central North America, central/eastern Europe into western Russia, and central South America (A6); hazards are shaped along virus‑specific ecological axes (A7); thermal and precipitation anomalies drive Old and New World lineages (e.g., Sin‑Nombre, Thailand virus) while urban‑centric profiles driven by fine‑scale anthropogenic changes affect Seoul and Mamore viruses (A8); climate change will restructure spillover across varied climatic and human‑dominated environments (A9).
analysis.contribution: The generated hazard maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the urgent need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness (A10).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; detailed methods, data sources, validation procedures, and uncertainty estimates are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover, exemplified by the 2026 multi‑country cruise‑ship Andes virus outbreak, and a macro‑ecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards has been lacking. Methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework used 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection modeling to project spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2‑4.5, SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060. Results: ~74.9% of land area is projected to have increased FOI, with three high‑hazard belts in western/central North America, central/eastern Europe into western Russia, and central South America; hazards align with virus‑specific ecological axes, with thermal/precipitation drivers for Old/New World lineages and urban‑centric profiles for Seoul and Mamore viruses. Contribution: The hazard maps offer a baseline for targeted global surveillance and underscore the need to merge spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness. Limitations: Only an abstract is available; detailed methods, data, validation, and uncertainty are not reported.

title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression

analysis.background: The 2025–2026 HPS epidemic in Argentina reached 101 confirmed cases, nearly double the previous season, with the epicenter shifting from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region.
analysis.methods: Case data (2020‑2026) with locations were combined via Google Earth Engine with ERA5‑Land temperature, SPI, and soil‑moisture anomalies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) evaluated 1‑6 month lag effects and spatial heterogeneity, and causal mediation analysis tested climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways.
analysis.results: Significant spatial clustering (Moran’s I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95 % CrI 1.31‑2.14) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature (RR = 1.45 per °C, 95 % CrI 1.12‑1.87) raised HPS risk, with interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months increased Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2 % and 56.7 % of effects.
analysis.contribution: First national‑scale quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies on hantavirus, providing a GEE‑based framework for a climate‑driven early‑warning system.
analysis.limitations: Only abstract information is available; full methods, validation, and data are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2025–2026 HPS epidemic in Argentina reached 101 confirmed cases, nearly double the previous season, with the epicenter shifting from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region. Methods: Case data (2020‑2026) with locations were combined via Google Earth Engine with ERA5‑Land temperature, SPI, and soil‑moisture anomalies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) evaluated 1‑6 month lag effects and spatial heterogeneity, and causal mediation analysis tested climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways. Results: Significant spatial clustering (Moran’s I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95 % CrI 1.31‑2.14) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature (RR = 1.45 per °C, 95 % CrI 1.12‑1.87) raised HPS risk, with interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months increased Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2 % and 56.7 % of effects. Contribution: First national‑scale quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies on hantavirus, providing a GEE‑based framework for a climate‑driven early‑warning system. Limitations: Only abstract information is available; full methods, validation, and data are not reported.

title: Neurological Manifestations of Hantavirus Infection: A Review.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses have caused recent human disease outbreaks, and their neurological complications are documented but remain poorly understood.
analysis.main_directions: Two clinical syndromes (pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal) are linked to hantavirus; neurological involvement is often secondary to hypoxic/ischemic or metabolic injury, but can include meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, and peripheral neuropathy.
analysis.current_state: Neurological manifestations are uncommon yet can be severe, usually secondary to systemic injury; no specific antivirals are approved and management is supportive care.
analysis.gaps: The pathophysiology, incidence, and optimal therapeutic approaches for hantavirus‑related neurological disease are unclear, and evidence is limited to case observations.
analysis.future_research: Prospective clinical studies and experimental models are needed to clarify mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and develop evidence‑based management guidelines.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks and neurological complications are poorly understood. Main directions: Two syndromes (pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal) are described; neurological disease may be secondary to hypoxia/ischemia or metabolic dysfunction, with occasional meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, or neuropathy. Current state: Neurological manifestations are uncommon but can be severe; they are often secondary to systemic injury, no approved antivirals exist, and care is supportive. Gaps: Pathophysiology and incidence remain unclear, and evidence for specific therapies is lacking. Future research: Prospective clinical studies and experimental models are required to delineate mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and develop management guidelines.

title: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.

analysis.background: Orthohantaviruses are a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 native rodent species serving as reservoirs for 15 viral genotypes, but their geographic distribution information is scattered, limiting risk assessment.
analysis.methods: The authors compiled occurrence records for reservoir hosts in SSA and applied ecological niche modeling to predict potential distributions; binary presence predictions were integrated to map reservoir and genotype richness and assemblages, and these richness maps were overlaid with gridded human population data to estimate exposed populations.
analysis.results: Most reservoir species are predicted across eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; hotspots of reservoir richness occur in the Humid Chaco, while high genotype richness spans the Chaco‑Pampean plain; exposure to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes was estimated to affect over 13 million people under the most precautionary scenario.
analysis.contribution: This work provides the first integrated, region‑wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, establishing an ecological baseline to inform surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is provided, so detailed methods, validation, and uncertainty estimates are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Orthohantaviruses pose a major zoonotic risk in Southern South America, with 13 rodent reservoirs for 15 genotypes, yet distribution data are fragmented. Methods: Occurrence records were compiled and ecological niche modeling used to predict host distributions; binary predictions mapped reservoir and genotype richness, which were combined with human population grids to estimate exposure. Results: Reservoirs are mainly in eastern/northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; richness hotspots lie in the Humid Chaco and genotype richness across the Chaco‑Pampean plain; >13 million people may be exposed to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes. Contribution: First region‑wide integrated assessment offers an ecological baseline for surveillance and risk mapping. Limitations: Only the abstract is available, so detailed methodological and uncertainty information is not reported.

title: IMMUNOINFORMATICS-GUIDED MULTI-EPITOPE VACCINE CANDIDATE DESIGN AND MHC DOCKING VALIDATION TARGETING THE GLYCOPROTEIN PRECURSOR OF THE NEWLY REPORTED ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 ORTHOHANTAVIRUS

analysis.background: The rapid release of viral genomic sequences enables early immunoinformatics screening before experimental antigen data are available (A1).
analysis.methods: The study applied a stepwise immunoinformatics workflow to the glycoprotein precursor of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus, encompassing antigenicity prediction, conserved‑region screening, transmembrane topology assessment, MHC‑I and MHC‑II epitope prediction, peptide‑construct design, and molecular docking to representative MHC receptors (A2).
analysis.results: VaxiJen scored the full GP as a probable antigen (0.5194) (A3); conserved regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 were retained as extracellular antigenic segments (A4); two CTL and six HTL epitopes passed MHC binding and antigenicity filters (A5); four constructs (F1‑F4) were assembled using linkers (A6); docking identified F4 as the strongest MHC‑I binder (score –226.92) and F2 as the strongest MHC‑II binder (score –260.14) (A7); contact‑map analysis showed supporting hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals interactions (A8).
analysis.contribution: The work prioritizes constructs F2 and F4 as leading multi‑epitope vaccine candidates for this novel orthohantavirus, informing subsequent 3‑D refinement, molecular dynamics, allergenicity/toxicity filtering, population‑coverage assessment, and experimental validation (A9).
analysis.limitations: Only abstract information is available; findings are limited to in‑silico predictions without experimental validation, and details on sample size, host, or geographic context are not reported (A1).

analysis.summary_en: Background: The rapid release of viral genomes permits early immunoinformatics screening before experimental antigen data exist (A1). Methods: A stepwise workflow evaluated the glycoprotein precursor of the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus, including antigenicity, conserved‑region, transmembrane, MHC‑I/II epitope prediction, construct design, and docking (A2). Results: VaxiJen gave the GP a score of 0.5194 (A3); regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 were selected as extracellular antigenic segments (A4); two CTL and six HTL epitopes passed filters (A5); four constructs (F1‑F4) were built (A6); docking ranked F4 best for MHC‑I (‑226.92) and F2 best for MHC‑II (‑260.14) (A7); contact‑map analysis confirmed stabilizing interactions (A8). Contribution: Constructs F2 and F4 are highlighted as priority vaccine candidates for further refinement and experimental testing (A9). Limitations: The report is abstract‑only, providing only computational results without experimental validation or detailed contextual data (A1).

title: Hantavirus infections in a changing world: epidemiology, pathogenesis, and one health challenges.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance with high morbidity, mortality, and complex ecological dynamics (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review focuses on rodent‑borne transmission, the two major clinical syndromes (HFRS and HCPS) (A2), the emerging urban epidemiology of Seoul virus (A5), and challenges in vaccine development, surveillance, and One Health integration (A6).
analysis.current_state: Current knowledge on transmission pathways, global epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and therapeutic options is summarised (A4).
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include limited vaccine candidates, insufficient surveillance systems, and the need to operationalise One Health approaches (A6), as well as the requirement for stronger multidisciplinary early‑detection frameworks (A7).
analysis.future_research: Future work should strengthen multidisciplinary collaborations, improve early detection and surveillance, and advance vaccine research within a One Health context (A7).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance with high morbidity, mortality, and complex ecological dynamics (A1). Main directions: The review addresses rodent transmission, HFRS and HCPS syndromes (A2), the rising urban spread of Seoul virus (A5), and challenges in vaccines, surveillance, and One Health (A6). Current state: Existing knowledge on transmission, epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical features, and treatment is summarised (A4). Gaps: Limited vaccine options, inadequate surveillance, and incomplete One Health implementation (A6) plus a need for stronger multidisciplinary early‑detection efforts (A7). Future research: Strengthen multidisciplinary approaches, enhance early detection and surveillance, and develop vaccines within a One Health framework (A7).

title: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia: a scoping review protocol with implications for surveillance

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are primarily hosted by rodents and other small mammals and can cause a spectrum of human disease from hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome to mild febrile illness.
analysis.main_directions: Disease patterns and viral circulation are shaped by virus‑host ecology, human exposure, diagnostics, and surveillance structures; a scoping review is proposed to map this heterogeneous literature.
analysis.current_state: Published data indicate hantavirus circulation in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, though the depth and type of evidence differ markedly among these countries.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is fragmented across clinical reports, serology, rodent and molecular studies, with many detections based on single serological assays and limited linked human‑animal surveillance; several regional countries have little or no published data.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced surveillance should incorporate molecular confirmation, integrated human‑animal studies, and systematic mapping of existing evidence to identify priority gaps.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are rodent‑borne and cause a range of human illnesses. Main directions: Their ecology, diagnostics, and surveillance drive disease patterns, prompting a scoping review of the scattered literature. Current state: Circulation is documented in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, but evidence quality varies. Gaps: Data are fragmented, often rely on single serology tests, and many countries lack published reports. Future research: Implement molecular confirmation, linked human‑animal surveillance, and comprehensive evidence mapping to guide surveillance priorities.

title: ANALYSIS OF GENOTYPE FREQUENCIES OF MONONUCLEOTIDE POLYMORPHISMS OF EARLY IMMUNE RESPONSE GENES IN HFRS PATIENTS

analysis.background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a leading focal infection in Russia with marked clinical variability.
analysis.methods: The study genotyped single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes in HFRS patients and a control group, using standard allele‑frequency determination techniques.
analysis.results: Genotype frequencies in controls followed Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, whereas HFRS patients showed a statistically significant deviation, indicating potential correlations between these SNPs and disease manifestations.
analysis.contribution: The findings suggest that polymorphisms in early immune‑response genes may influence HFRS clinical outcomes, providing a basis for further genetic surveillance and risk stratification.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a leading focal infection in Russia with marked clinical variability. Methods: The study genotyped SNPs in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes in HFRS patients and controls. Results: Control genotypes conformed to Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, while HFRS patients showed significant deviation, suggesting correlations. Contribution: Polymorphisms in early immune‑response genes may influence HFRS clinical outcomes, informing surveillance and risk assessment. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: A 5-Adic Ultrametric Framework for Alignment-Free Phylogenetic Analysis of Hantavirus RNA Sequences

analysis.background: The paper addresses the need for alignment‑free phylogenetic analysis of Hantavirus RNA sequences by proposing a novel mathematical framework.
analysis.methods: An injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic embedding maps RNA sequences into a compact ultrametric space of 5‑adic integers (A2); a finite‑resolution projection onto quotient rings of Z5 and a 5‑adic valuation‑based distance are used for phylogenetic inference (A6, A7).
analysis.results: The embedding is proved to be a global isometry linking a symbolic prefix metric to the 5‑adic metric, with its image forming a compact Cantor‑type subset of Z5 (A3); a continuous‑time evolutionary model based on a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential operator yields a diffusion equation with an explicit fundamental solution via 5‑adic Fourier analysis (A4, A5); the induced ultrametric distance naturally encodes hierarchical relationships (A7); the framework is illustrated on Hantavirus genomic sequences (A9).
analysis.contribution: The work bridges p‑adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, pseudo‑differential operators, and computational phylogenetics, providing a mathematically rigorous, alignment‑free approach for hierarchical evolutionary inference (A8, A9).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; no empirical data, performance evaluation, or validation on actual Hantavirus datasets are reported (abstract_only).

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study proposes a new alignment‑free phylogenetic method for Hantavirus RNA using a 5‑adic framework. Methods: RNA sequences are embedded into a 5‑adic ultrametric space via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic map and analyzed with a valuation‑based distance (A2, A6, A7). Results: The embedding is a global isometry forming a Cantor‑type subset; a Vladimirov‑operator diffusion model yields an explicit solution; the ultrametric distance captures hierarchical relationships; illustration on Hantavirus sequences demonstrates feasibility (A3‑A5, A7, A9). Contribution: Links p‑adic mathematics to phylogenetics, offering a rigorous, alignment‑free hierarchical inference tool (A8, A9). Limitations: Only abstract information is available; no empirical validation or quantitative performance data are reported.

title: Serologic Evidence of Hantavirus Exposure in Humans, Italy, 2004-2018.

analysis.background: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed; this study aimed to determine serologic evidence of exposure in northwestern Italy.
analysis.methods: 371 patient samples collected 2004–2018 from Novara, Biella, Vercelli, and Verbano Cusio‑Ossola were tested for PUUV, DOBV, and LCMV IgG by immunofluorescence assay, with neutralizing antibodies assessed by focus‑reduction neutralization tests in 2024–2025.
analysis.results: Hantavirus IgG was detected in 7.3% (27/371) of samples and LCMV IgG in 2.7% (10/371); 10 of 11 IgG‑positive samples had PUUV‑neutralizing activity (titers 1:80–>1:320).
analysis.contribution: The study provides the first serologic evidence of hantavirus and LCMV exposure in this previously unstudied region, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation and a higher seroprevalence than reported elsewhere.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; the sample comprised patients with chronic diseases, limiting generalizability.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed; this study aimed to determine serologic evidence of exposure in northwestern Italy. Methods: 371 patient samples collected 2004–2018 from Novara, Biella, Vercelli, and Verbano Cusio‑Ossola were tested for PUUV, DOBV, and LCMV IgG by immunofluorescence assay, with neutralizing antibodies assessed by focus‑reduction neutralization tests in 2024–2025. Results: Hantavirus IgG was detected in 7.3% (27/371) of samples and LCMV IgG in 2.7% (10/371); 10 of 11 IgG‑positive samples had PUUV‑neutralizing activity (titers 1:80–>1:320). Contribution: The study provides the first serologic evidence of hantavirus and LCMV exposure in this previously unstudied region, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation and a higher seroprevalence than reported elsewhere. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; the sample comprised patients with chronic diseases, limiting generalizability.

title: The 2026 Andes virus outbreak: What genomic stability reveals about virulence.

analysis.background: The outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) on an international cruise ship raised concerns because hantaviruses are high‑mortality pathogens (A1) and ANDV causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with case‑fatality rates of 25‑40% (F3, F4).
analysis.methods: The report does not provide detailed study design, sample collection, or analytical methods; only a brief mention of the MV Hondius outbreak is given (A2).
analysis.results: Thirteen cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and three deaths were reported (F5); genomic analysis showed identical S and M segments and only two synonymous SNPs in the L segment across cases (A17); the 2026 strain lacked previously identified mutations linked to person‑to‑person transmission (A22, A24).

title: t-PA/PAI-1 complex expression correlates with the severity in hantaan virus-induced hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome

analysis.background: The study investigates whether the tissue plasminogen activator/PAI‑1 complex (t‑PAIC) can serve as a biomarker for the severity of hantaan virus‑induced hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS).
analysis.methods: A cohort of 156 HFRS patients and 37 healthy controls was examined; t‑PAIC was measured by chemiluminescence immunoassay, routine labs (WBC, PLT, Cr, UA, urea, HDL, ALB, APTT, PT) and HFRS‑specific antibodies were assessed, and statistical analyses (Spearman correlation, ordinal logistic regression, ROC) were performed.
analysis.results: t‑PAIC levels increased with HFRS severity, peaking in critically ill patients; they positively correlated with leukocytes, creatinine, urea, UA, APTT, PT and negatively with platelets, HDL, ALB. ROC analysis showed an AUC of 0.796 (95 % CI 0.724–0.867).
analysis.contribution: t‑PAIC reflects renal dysfunction and coagulation abnormalities, suggesting its use as a biomarker for monitoring disease status in HFRS.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) caused by hantaan virus remains a public health threat; this study examined whether the tissue plasminogen activator/PAI‑1 complex (t‑PAIC) could serve as a biomarker of disease severity. Methods: 156 HFRS patients and 37 healthy controls were enrolled; t‑PAIC was quantified by chemiluminescence immunoassay, and routine labs (WBC, PLT, Cr, UA, urea, HDL, ALB, APTT, PT) and HFRS‑specific antibodies were measured. Spearman correlation, ordinal logistic regression, and ROC analysis evaluated associations and predictive performance. Results: t‑PAIC rose progressively with severity, peaking in critically ill patients; it correlated positively with leukocytes, creatinine, urea, UA, APTT, PT and inversely with platelets, HDL, ALB. ROC yielded AUC = 0.796 (95 % CI 0.724–0.867). Contribution: t‑PAIC reflects renal dysfunction and coagulation derangements, offering a potential tool for monitoring HFRS severity. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Cruise-linked Andes virus and severe hantavirus lung disease: A narrative review

title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely exhibits repeated human‑to‑human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America.
analysis.main_directions: The review synthesizes epidemiological and clinical features, transmission routes, and patterns of spread in a confined, high‑density setting, using the 2026 MV Hondius cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study to inform future public‑health response strategies.
analysis.current_state: Genomic sequencing showed ≤1 SNP difference among cases, indicating a single or few zoonotic introductions followed by onboard human‑to‑human spread, with an estimated effective reproduction number of 0.7.
analysis.gaps: Uncertainty remains about the precise conditions that enable limited‑transmissibility pathogens to amplify in closed environments and the generalizability of these findings beyond this single outbreak.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced preparedness should include systematic travel‑history assessment, active surveillance for suspected cases, and deployment of rapid diagnostic testing to detect and contain similar events.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and causes lethal cardiopulmonary syndrome. Main directions: The review collates epidemiology, clinical features, transmission routes, and confined‑environment dynamics, using the 2026 cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study for future response. Current state: Genomic data show ≤1 SNP difference among cases, supporting a single or few spillovers with an R ≈ 0.7. Gaps: Conditions that allow limited‑transmissibility pathogens to amplify in closed settings remain unclear and may not generalize. Future research: Strengthen preparedness via travel‑history screening, active case surveillance, and rapid diagnostics.

title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles global biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover, highlighted by the 2026 multi‑country cruise ship outbreak of Andes virus, yet macroecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards has been lacking.
analysis.methods: The study integrated 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection (FOI) modeling and machine‑learning to project global spillover hazards under current climate and future scenarios (SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060.
analysis.results: Models show ~74.9 % of modeled land area will experience increased FOI, identifying three high‑hazard belts in temperate North America, Europe‑Russia, and central South America; thermal and precipitation anomalies drive Old/New World lineages, while urban landscape changes shape Seoul and Mamore virus risks.
analysis.contribution: The generated hazard maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and emphasize integrating spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available; detailed methodology, data sources, and validation are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover, exemplified by the 2026 Andes virus cruise‑ship outbreak, yet macroecological synthesis for hantavirus risk is lacking. Methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework combined 89 virus‑host links across 61 reservoirs with force‑of‑infection modeling to project hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2‑4.5, SSP5‑8.5) for 2041‑2060. Results: ~74.9% of land area is projected to see increased FOI, with three high‑hazard belts in temperate North America, Europe‑Russia, and central South America; thermal/precipitation anomalies drive Old/New World lineages, while urban landscape changes affect Seoul and Mamore viruses. Contribution: Hazard maps offer a baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the need to align conservation with public‑health preparedness. Limitations: Only the abstract is provided; detailed methods, data, and validation are not reported.

title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.

analysis.background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses shows variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding, yet no systematic synthesis of haemostatic dysfunction existed.
analysis.main_directions: The review compares thrombocytopenia severity across HTNV, DOBV, SEOV versus PUUV, notes mild APTT prolongation and D‑dimer elevation, highlights a spectrum of bleeding from epistaxis to intracranial haemorrhage, and points out limited paediatric data suggesting milder disease.
analysis.current_state: Thrombocytopenia is the most consistent laboratory abnormality, especially in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections; coagulation time changes and D‑dimer rises are reported variably; low platelet counts dominate clinical observations.
analysis.gaps: Study quality ranges from low to moderate, reporting is incomplete, and heterogeneity in outcome definitions hampers firm conclusions about haemostatic mechanisms.
analysis.future_research: Prospective, standardised investigations of coagulation parameters and bleeding outcomes are needed, alongside harmonised reporting to clarify pathogenesis and improve patient management.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses shows variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding, yet no systematic synthesis existed. Main directions: The review compares thrombocytopenia severity across HTNV, DOBV, SEOV versus PUUV, notes mild APTT prolongation and D‑dimer elevation, outlines bleeding from mild to severe, and highlights limited paediatric data. Current state: Thrombocytopenia is the most consistent finding, especially with HTNV/DOBV/SEOV; coagulation time changes and D‑dimer rises are variably reported; low platelets dominate clinical observations. Gaps: Variable study quality, incomplete reporting, and heterogeneous outcome definitions limit conclusions. Future research: Prospective, standardised studies of coagulation and bleeding, with harmonised reporting, are required to clarify mechanisms and guide care.

title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression

analysis.background: Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) cases in Argentina rose to 101 in the 2025–2026 season, nearly doubling the previous season, with a notable shift of the epidemic epicenter from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region.
analysis.methods: Confirmed HPS case data (2020‑2026) with geographic and temporal details were combined with ERA5‑Land and NOAA GSOD climate variables processed via Google Earth Engine; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression with INLA estimated lagged climate effects, and causal mediation analysis examined climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways.
analysis.results: Spatial clustering was significant (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI 1.31–2.14) lagged 3‑4 months and temperature anomalies (RR = 1.45 per °C, 95% CrI 1.12–1.87) positively affected HPS onset with synergistic interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months after SPI > 1.5 raised Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, while drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2% (Buenos Aires) and 56.7% (NOA) of climate‑HPS risk.
analysis.contribution: The study provides the first national‑scale quantitative causal inference linking climate anomalies to hantavirus outbreaks in Argentina and proposes a GEE‑based real‑time monitoring framework for an early‑warning system.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available, limiting detail on data quality, model validation, and potential confounders.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HPS cases surged to 101 in 2025–2026, nearly doubling the prior season, with the epicenter shifting to Buenos Aires and the NOA region. Methods: Confirmed case data (2020‑2026) were merged with ERA5‑Land and NOAA climate variables via Google Earth Engine; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) assessed lagged effects, and causal mediation analysis explored climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways. Results: Significant spatial clustering (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68) lagged 3‑4 months and temperature (RR = 1.45 per °C) increased HPS risk, with synergistic interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood months after high SPI raised Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold, drought‑followed‑heavy‑rainfall drove NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2%–56.7% of risk. Contribution: First national‑scale causal inference of climate‑driven hantavirus outbreaks and a GEE‑based early‑warning framework. Limitations: Only abstract provided, restricting methodological and validation details.

title: Neurological Manifestations of Hantavirus Infection: A Review.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses have caused several recent human disease outbreaks, prompting review of their clinical spectrum.
analysis.main_directions: The review outlines two major clinical syndromes—pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome—and describes associated neurological manifestations ranging from secondary hypoxic injury to meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, and peripheral neuropathy.
analysis.current_state: Neurological complications of hantavirus infection are documented but remain poorly understood, and they are considered uncommon yet potentially severe.
analysis.gaps: Lack of specific antiviral therapies and limited prospective clinical data leave the pathophysiology, optimal management, and therapeutic targets inadequately characterized.
analysis.future_research: Prospective cohort studies and experimental models are needed to delineate neurological manifestations, clarify mechanisms, identify treatment targets, and develop management guidelines.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks, driving interest in their disease spectrum. Current state: Neurological complications are documented but poorly understood and can be severe despite being uncommon. Gaps: No approved antivirals and scarce prospective data limit understanding of pathophysiology and treatment. Future research: Prospective studies and experimental models should characterize manifestations, mechanisms, therapeutic targets, and management guidelines.

title: HantaWatch: Federated Learning for Hantavirus Genomic Surveillance

analysis.background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is constrained by dispersed sequence data, non‑IID heterogeneity, and limited expert‑review capacity (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The authors propose HantaWatch, a federated learning framework that lets labs train sequence‑based models collaboratively without sharing raw data (A2).
analysis.current_state: HantaWatch combines k‑mer extraction, source‑aware client construction, adaptive DU‑FedProx optimization, task‑specific model selection, and prediction‑only triage (A3).
analysis.gaps: The abstract reports experimental performance but does not provide real‑world deployment results, external validation, or assessment of integration with public‑health workflows (A4).
analysis.future_research: Further work should evaluate HantaWatch in operational surveillance settings, quantify its impact on expert prioritization, and refine risk‑score calibration and uncertainty handling (A6).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is limited by distributed data, heterogeneity, and expert capacity (A1). Main directions: HantaWatch is a federated learning framework enabling collaborative model training without raw data sharing (A2). Current state: It integrates k‑mer features, source‑aware clients, adaptive DU‑FedProx, task‑specific selection, and prediction‑only triage (A3). Gaps: Experimental results lack real‑world validation and workflow integration evidence (A4). Future research: Deploy and assess HantaWatch in operational settings, measure its effect on expert prioritization, and improve risk‑score and uncertainty calibration (A6).

title: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.

analysis.background: Orthohantaviruses represent a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 native rodent species serving as reservoirs for 15 viral genotypes, and existing distribution information is fragmented, limiting risk assessment (A1; A2).
analysis.methods: The study compiled occurrence records for reservoir hosts, applied ecological niche modeling to estimate potential distributions, integrated binary presence predictions to map reservoir and genotype richness, and combined richness maps with gridded human population data to estimate exposure (A3; A4; A5).
analysis.results: Most reservoir species are distributed in eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; hotspot of reservoir richness is in the Humid Chaco, while high genotype richness extends across the Chaco‑Pampean plain; exposure to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes was estimated to exceed 13 million people under the most precautionary scenario (A6; A7; A8).
analysis.contribution: The paper provides the first integrated, region‑wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, offering an updated ecological baseline to support surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies (A9).
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available; detailed methods, validation, and uncertainty are not reported (not reported in the supplied evidence).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Orthohantaviruses are a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 rodent species hosting 15 genotypes, but distribution data are fragmented. Methods: Occurrence records were compiled, ecological niche modeling estimated host distributions, binary presence maps assessed reservoir and genotype richness, and these were overlaid with human population grids. Results: Reservoirs mainly occur in eastern/northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; reservoir richness hotspots lie in the Humid Chaco, genotype richness spans the Chaco‑Pampean plain, and >13 million people may be exposed to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes under a precautionary scenario. Contribution: This first region‑wide integrated assessment supplies an ecological baseline for surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention. Limitations: Only the abstract is available; detailed methods, validation, and uncertainty are not reported.

title: IMMUNOINFORMATICS-GUIDED MULTI-EPITOPE VACCINE CANDIDATE DESIGN AND MHC DOCKING VALIDATION TARGETING THE GLYCOPROTEIN PRECURSOR OF THE NEWLY REPORTED ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 ORTHOHANTAVIRUS

analysis.background: The study addresses the need for rapid vaccine candidate identification for the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus, leveraging the quick availability of viral genome sequences.
analysis.methods: The authors applied an immunoinformatics workflow that included antigenicity prediction (VaxiJen), conserved‑region screening, transmembrane topology analysis, MHC‑I and MHC‑II epitope prediction, peptide‑construct design with AAY/GPGPG linkers, and molecular docking against representative MHC receptors.
analysis.results: The GP protein scored 0.5194 in VaxiJen, with antigenic conserved regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 outside membrane helices; two CTL and six HTL epitopes were selected, yielding four constructs (F1‑F4); docking identified F4 as best for MHC‑I (‑226.92) and F2 as best for MHC‑II (‑260.14), with interaction analyses showing hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, alkyl/aliphatic contacts, and van der Waals forces.
analysis.contribution: The work provides a prioritized set of multi‑epitope vaccine candidates (F2, F4) and demonstrates a rapid in‑silico pipeline for orthohantavirus vaccine design, guiding subsequent refinement and experimental testing.
analysis.limitations: Only abstract data are provided; no experimental validation, population‑coverage analysis, or allergenicity/toxicity assessment is reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study targets rapid vaccine design for the newly reported ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 orthohantavirus using early genomic data. Methods: An immunoinformatics pipeline integrated VaxiJen antigenicity, conserved‑region screening, transmembrane topology, MHC‑I/II epitope prediction, construct design with linkers, and MHC docking. Results: GP scored 0.5194; antigenic regions 1‑50 and 81‑240 were selected, yielding two CTL and six HTL epitopes assembled into four constructs (F1‑F4). Docking ranked F4 highest for MHC‑I (‑226.92) and F2 for MHC‑II (‑260.14), with supportive hydrogen bonds, salt bridges, and van der Waals contacts. Contribution: Prioritizes F2 and F4 as vaccine candidates and showcases a fast in‑silico design workflow. Limitations: Findings are limited to abstract‑only information; no experimental or broader immunological validation is presented.

title: Hantavirus infections in a changing world: epidemiology, pathogenesis, and one health challenges.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics.
analysis.main_directions: They are transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, causing haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS), with emerging urban-associated Seoul virus (SEOV) linked to brown rats and pet rodents.
analysis.current_state: Recent environmental changes, increased human–animal interaction, and global mobility have shifted epidemiological patterns, and current knowledge covers transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches.
analysis.gaps: Challenges remain in vaccine development, surveillance, and integrating One Health strategies for prevention and control.
analysis.future_research: Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection are critical to mitigating the global impact of hantavirus infections.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics. Current state: Recent environmental changes, increased human–animal interaction, and global mobility have shifted epidemiological patterns, and current knowledge covers transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiology, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches. Gaps: Challenges remain in vaccine development, surveillance, and integrating One Health strategies for prevention and control. Future research: Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection are critical to mitigating the global impact of hantavirus infections.

title: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia: a scoping review protocol with implications for surveillance

analysis.background: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia is fragmented across clinical, serological, rodent, molecular, and surveillance reports, prompting a review to inform surveillance.
analysis.main_directions: The review will map disease manifestations (HF with renal syndrome, HPS, etc.), ecological drivers (virus‑host species, local ecology, exposure, diagnostics, surveillance), and the suitability of a scoping approach for heterogeneous literature.
analysis.current_state: Published data indicate hantavirus circulation in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, though evidence quantity and type differ markedly among countries; many detections rely on single serological assays with limited molecular confirmation or linked human‑animal studies.
analysis.gaps: Molecular confirmation and integrated human‑animal surveillance are uncommon; several countries have scant or no published data; lack of publications does not prove absence of circulation.
analysis.future_research: The scoping review will delineate studied topics, geographic coverage, assessment methods, and prioritize surveillance gaps to guide targeted epidemiological and ecological investigations.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia is fragmented across clinical, serological, rodent, molecular, and surveillance reports, prompting a review to inform surveillance. Main directions: The review will map disease manifestations (HF with renal syndrome, HPS, etc.), ecological drivers (virus‑host species, local ecology, exposure, diagnostics, surveillance), and the suitability of a scoping approach for heterogeneous literature. title: ANALYSIS OF GENOTYPE FREQUENCIES OF MONONUCLEOTIDE POLYMORPHISMS OF EARLY IMMUNE RESPONSE GENES IN HFRS PATIENTS

analysis.background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a leading natural focal infection in Russia with pronounced clinical variability.
analysis.methods: The study determined allele and genotype frequencies of SNPs in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes, as clinical manifestations of HFRS may be influenced by such polymorphisms.
analysis.results: In HFRS patients, genotype frequencies deviated significantly from Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, whereas the control group conformed to the expected equilibrium, indicating a potential association between these SNPs and disease manifestations.
analysis.contribution: The findings suggest that early immune response gene polymorphisms may contribute to the clinical variability observed in HFRS patients.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; the study is presented as a conference abstract with limited methodological detail.

title: A 5-Adic Ultrametric Framework for Alignment-Free Phylogenetic Analysis of Hantavirus RNA Sequences

analysis.background: The paper tackles the need for alignment‑free phylogenetic methods for viral RNA, exemplified by Hantavirus, by proposing a non‑Archimedean mathematical framework.
analysis.methods: RNA sequences are mapped to points in the 5‑adic integer ring via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic embedding, with finite‑resolution projection onto quotient rings and a 5‑adic valuation‑based ultrametric distance used for phylogenetic inference.
analysis.results: The embedding is proved to be a global isometry whose image forms a compact Cantor‑type subset of Z5; a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential diffusion equation with an explicit fundamental solution is derived, and the induced ultrametric distance naturally encodes hierarchical relationships, illustrated on Hantavirus genomes.
analysis.contribution: Establishes a novel bridge between p‑adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, and computational phylogenetics, offering a mathematically rigorous alignment‑free framework for hierarchical evolutionary analysis.
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; no empirical validation, performance comparison, or detailed data are reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study addresses the need for alignment‑free phylogenetic analysis of viral RNA, using Hantavirus as an example. Methods: RNA sequences are embedded into the 5‑adic integer ring via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic map, with finite‑resolution projection and a 5‑adic valuation‑based ultrametric distance for inference. Results: The embedding is a global isometry forming a compact Cantor‑type subset; a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential diffusion equation with an explicit solution is derived, and the ultrametric distance encodes hierarchical relationships, demonstrated on Hantavirus genomes. Contribution: Provides a novel, mathematically rigorous bridge between p‑adic analysis and computational phylogenetics for alignment‑free evolutionary studies. Limitations: Only an abstract is available; no empirical validation or detailed data are reported.

title: Serologic Evidence of Hantavirus Exposure in Humans, Italy, 2004-2018.

analysis.background: Rodent‑borne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed.
analysis.methods: Samples from 371 patients collected 2004–2018 in the provinces of Novara, Biella, Vercelli, and Verbano‑Cusio‑Ossola were screened for hantavirus and LCMV IgG by immunofluorescence assay; IgG‑positive samples with titers >1:40 were further examined by focus‑reduction neutralization tests in Vero E6 cells to detect PUUV and DOBV neutralizing activity.
analysis.results: Hantavirus IgG was found in 27/371 (7.3%, 95% CI 5.0%–10.4%); PUUV‑neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 of 11 tested samples (titers 1:80 to >1:320); LCMV IgG was present in 10/371 (2.7%, 95% CI 1.5%–4.9%).
analysis.contribution: The study reveals unrecognized local PUUV circulation and measurable LCMV exposure in a previously understudied region of northwestern Italy, showing seroprevalence higher than national averages and informing surveillance and public‑health awareness.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Rodent‑borne hantaviruses and LCMV circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed. Methods: 371 patient samples from 2004–2018 were screened for IgG by IFA and positive samples were tested by FRNT in Vero E6 cells for PUUV and DOBV neutralization. Results: 7.3% were hantavirus IgG positive; PUUV‑neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 of 11 tested samples; 2.7% were LCMV IgG positive. Contribution: Demonstrates unrecognized local PUUV circulation and notable LCMV exposure, indicating higher seroprevalence than national estimates and guiding surveillance. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: The 2026 Andes virus outbreak: What genomic stability reveals about virulence.

analysis.background: A new hantavirus outbreak on an international cruise ship prompted concern about a possible novel genetic variant of a high‑mortality pathogen.
analysis.methods: Genomic sequencing of virus isolates from the 13 reported cases revealed identical S and M segments and only two synonymous SNPs in the L segment, with comparison to prior ANDV isolates.
analysis.results: All 2026 cases carried a genetically stable virus in clade 3, lacking mutations previously linked to person‑to‑person transmission; 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) resulted in 3 deaths.
analysis.contribution: The findings underscore that virulence and transmissibility of Andes virus are not driven by recent genetic changes, highlighting the need to reassess epidemiological risk based on genomic stability.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: A new hantavirus outbreak on an international cruise ship prompted concern about a possible novel genetic variant of a high‑mortality pathogen. Methods: Genomic sequencing of virus isolates from the 13 reported cases revealed identical S and M segments and only two synonymous SNPs in the L segment, with comparison to prior ANDV isolates. Results: All 2026 cases carried a genetically stable virus in clade 3, lacking mutations previously linked to person‑to‑person transmission; 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) resulted in 3 deaths. Contribution: The findings underscore that virulence and transmissibility of Andes virus are not driven by recent genetic changes, highlighting the need to reassess epidemiological risk based on genomic stability. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Hantavirus on a Nature Cruise and the Risk of a Pandemic.

analysis.background: The Viewpoint was prompted by the alarm over hantavirus cases first reported in May 2026 and the broader concern about emerging zoonoses such as Ebola.
analysis.main_directions: It outlines the hantavirus outbreak on a nature cruise, evaluates pandemic potential, and situates the event within the context of other high‑risk viral threats.
analysis.current_state: Only early case reports are available, providing limited insight into transmission dynamics, clinical severity, and reservoir species.
analysis.gaps: Epidemiological data are sparse, the source of infection on the cruise is unclear, and systematic surveillance of travel‑associated hantavirus is lacking.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced genomic surveillance, detailed outbreak investigations, risk modeling for cruise‑ship transmission, and development of mitigation guidelines for zoonotic spillover during travel are needed.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The Viewpoint responds to the May 2026 hantavirus cases and wider emerging viral threats. Main directions: It discusses the cruise‑ship outbreak, pandemic risk, and parallels with other zoonoses. title: Artificial intelligence for chest imaging in hantavirus pulmonary syndrome: A review of practical considerations for rare-disease imaging.

analysis.background: HPS is a rare zoonotic respiratory illness with a high case fatality rate.
analysis.main_directions: Travel‑associated Andes orthohantavirus clusters have renewed interest in early radiographic recognition outside endemic regions, and the review outlines imaging features and AI development considerations such as dataset creation, label standardisation, realistic comparators, modelling approaches, external validation, and workflow integration.
analysis.current_state: AI tools that proved useful for COVID‑19 imaging now inform radiology workflows, but their application to HPS chest imaging remains largely unexplored.
analysis.gaps: Key challenges are data scarcity, low pretest probability in non‑endemic settings, overlapping imaging findings with other viral pneumonias, and translational pitfalls highlighted in recent AI literature.
analysis.future_research: Future work should pursue multi‑institutional, prevalence‑aware datasets, robust external validation, and multimodal integration of imaging with clinical, laboratory, and epidemiologic information to develop reliable HPS‑aware AI systems.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HPS is a rare zoonotic respiratory illness with high fatality. Main directions: Recent travel‑associated Andes orthohantavirus clusters have spurred interest in early radiographic detection beyond endemic areas, and the review details imaging features and AI development needs. Current state: AI successful in COVID‑19 imaging exists, yet its use for HPS remains largely unexplored. Gaps: Data scarcity, low pretest probability, overlapping imaging patterns, and translational pitfalls limit progress. Future research: Assemble multi‑institutional, prevalence‑aware datasets, perform external validation, and integrate imaging with clinical and epidemiologic data for robust HPS AI tools.

title: Hantavirus - Once Again in the News.
The Hantavirus Paradigm: Genomic Architecture, Epidemiological Dynamics, Diagnostic Advances and Mitigation Strategies

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are rodent‑borne zoonotic viruses of the family Hantaviridae and are classified as significant emerging diseases globally (A1).
analysis.methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence.
analysis.results: Recent developments in epidemiological studies, genomic surveillance, and molecular diagnostics have improved knowledge of hantavirus diversity, transmission dynamics, and illness pathophysiology (A4). Problems with early diagnosis, efficient treatment, and epidemic prevention still exist (A5).
analysis.contribution: The review compiles current understanding of hantavirus epidemiology, clinical signs, diagnostic techniques, and preventive strategies (A6) and highlights new developments, ongoing studies, and potential avenues for better risk assessment, surveillance, and public‑health initiatives (A7).
analysis.limitations: The abstract notes persistent challenges in early diagnosis, treatment, and prevention (A5) and provides only abstract‑level information (abstract_only).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are rodent‑borne zoonotic viruses classified as significant emerging diseases worldwide (A1). Methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence. Contribution: This review synthesizes current epidemiology, clinical presentation, diagnostics, and prevention, and points to new research directions to improve risk assessment, surveillance, and public‑health actions (A6, A7). title: Hantavirus Infection: Current Epidemiology, Emerging Trends, and One Health Perspectives

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are globally distributed zoonotic RNA viruses maintained in rodent, insectivore, and bat reservoirs, with human infection mainly via inhalation of aerosolized excreta (A1).
analysis.methods: The paper is a review that synthesizes current evidence on hantavirus taxonomy, epidemiology, transmission, pathogenesis, diagnosis, clinical management, vaccines, and prevention within a One Health framework (A6).
analysis.results: Surveillance and seroprevalence data suggest the true disease burden is underestimated because subclinical infection and clinical overlap with other febrile illnesses limit recognition (A3); recent trends include climate‑driven changes in rodent dynamics, land‑use expansion of human‑rodent interfaces, and concern about Andes virus, the only orthohantavirus with documented person‑to‑person transmission (A4).
analysis.contribution: The review integrates human, animal, and environmental surveillance to inform control strategies and highlights gaps such as the lack of broadly licensed antivirals and limited vaccine access (A5, A6).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is available; detailed search strategy, inclusion criteria, and quantitative data are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are globally distributed zoonotic RNA viruses transmitted to humans mainly via aerosolized rodent excreta (A1). Methods: This review synthesizes existing evidence on hantavirus biology and control within a One Health framework (A6). Results: Surveillance indicates substantial underestimation of disease burden due to subclinical infection and overlap with other febrile illnesses (A3); recent trends show climate‑driven reservoir changes, expanded human‑rodent interfaces, and concern over Andes virus person‑to‑person spread (A4). Contribution: The work highlights integrated surveillance needs and the absence of widely available antivirals or vaccines (A5, A6). Limitations: Only the abstract is provided; detailed methods and data are not reported (not reported in the supplied evidence).

title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome and has repeatedly shown human‑to‑human transmission, prompting review of its outbreak potential.
analysis.main_directions: The review synthesizes epidemiology, clinical presentation, transmission routes, especially in confined high‑density settings, and evaluates public‑health response strategies using the 2026 MV Hondensis cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study.
analysis.current_state: Genomic sequencing showed ≤1 SNP among cases, supporting a single or few zoonotic introductions followed by onboard human‑to‑human spread, with an estimated effective reproduction number of 0.7.
analysis.gaps: Uncertainty remains regarding the precise transmissibility parameters in such environments, as the modest R = 0.7 estimate is derived from limited case numbers and may not capture superspreading potential.
analysis.future_research: Enhanced surveillance of travel‑associated cases, routine assessment of exposure history, and deployment of rapid diagnostic assays are recommended to improve early detection and containment of ANDV in similar settings.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome and has repeatedly shown human‑to‑human transmission, prompting review of its outbreak potential. Main directions: The review synthesizes epidemiology, clinical presentation, transmission routes, especially in confined high‑density settings, and evaluates public‑health response strategies using the 2026 MV Hondensis cruise‑ship outbreak as a case study. Current state: Genomic sequencing showed ≤1 SNP among cases, supporting a single or few zoonotic introductions followed by onboard human‑to‑human spread, with an estimated effective reproduction number of 0.7. Gaps: Uncertainty remains regarding the precise transmissibility parameters in such environments, as the modest R = 0.7 estimate is derived from limited case numbers and may not capture superspreading potential. Future research: Enhanced surveillance of travel‑associated cases, routine assessment of exposure history, and deployment of rapid diagnostic assays are recommended to improve early detection and containment of ANDV in similar settings.

title: Spectrum of Gastrointestinal, Hepatobiliary, and Pancreatic Involvement in Hantavirus Infection: A Narrative Review.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infection is a zoonotic, rodent‑transmitted disease causing hemorrhagic fever with renal failure and pulmonary syndrome, prompting renewed interest after recent outbreaks.
analysis.main_directions: The review highlights gastrointestinal, hepatobiliary, and pancreatic manifestations—early prodromal GI symptoms, transaminitis, gallbladder changes, and pancreatitis—and discusses supportive care and limited antiviral options.
analysis.current_state: GI symptoms are among the earliest and most frequent signs, with hepatic involvement usually limited to transient aminotransferase elevations, while management remains largely supportive.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is sparse, derived mainly from case reports and small retrospective studies, limiting understanding of incidence, mechanisms, and prognostic impact of these extra‑renal manifestations.
analysis.future_research: Large prospective studies are required to define mechanisms, true incidence, and prognostic significance of GI and hepatopancreatic involvement in hantavirus infection.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis causing severe renal and pulmonary disease, recently highlighted by outbreaks. Main directions: The review surveys GI, liver, gallbladder, and pancreatic signs, noting early nausea, vomiting, transaminitis, gallbladder wall thickening, and pancreatitis, with care focused on supportive measures and limited antivirals. Current state: Early GI symptoms are common; hepatic changes are usually mild; management is supportive. Gaps: Data are limited to case reports and small studies, leaving incidence and prognostic relevance unclear. Future research: Prospective, large‑scale studies are needed to clarify mechanisms, frequency, and outcome implications of these manifestations.

title: Hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in children: a systematic scoping review of presentation, critical care management, and outcomes.

analysis.background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction, and pediatric‑specific evidence is limited.
analysis.main_directions: Common presentations include fever, GI symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock; severe cases require mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO, and sometimes renal replacement therapy; mortality rates are 33‑37%.
analysis.current_state: The literature is dominated by case reports and surveillance data with variable completeness, but consensus exists on the high mortality and need for advanced support.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is sparse, largely case‑based, with inconsistent reporting, limiting generalizability and understanding of risk factors.
analysis.future_research: Prospective surveillance, standardized reporting, and studies evaluating early recognition, supportive care protocols, and advanced cardiopulmonary support in pediatric HCPS/HPS are needed.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction, and pediatric‑specific evidence is limited. Main directions: Common presentations include fever, GI symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock; severe cases require mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO, and sometimes renal replacement therapy; mortality rates are 33‑37%. Current state: The literature is dominated by case reports and surveillance data with variable completeness, but consensus exists on the high mortality and need for advanced support. Gaps: Evidence is sparse, largely case‑based, with inconsistent reporting, limiting generalizability and understanding of risk factors. Future research: Prospective surveillance, standardized reporting, and studies evaluating early recognition, supportive care protocols, and advanced cardiopulmonary support in pediatric HCPS/HPS are needed.

title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising concerns for post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
analysis.main_directions: PEP relevance for high‑risk household, healthcare, or laboratory exposures is questioned due to ANDV’s high fatality.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal antiviral activity, but clinical evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited.
analysis.gaps: Lack of controlled human studies and potential toxicity restrict ribavirin PEP to highly selected scenarios and early use.
analysis.future_research: Model‑based dosing strategies and clinical trials are needed to define safe, effective ribavirin PEP regimens.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising concerns for post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Main directions: PEP relevance for high‑risk household, healthcare, or laboratory exposures is questioned due to ANDV’s high fatality. Current state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal antiviral activity, but clinical evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited. Gaps: Lack of controlled human studies and potential toxicity restrict ribavirin PEP to highly selected scenarios and early use. title: First nationwide full-genome characterisation of human-derived Andes virus in Chile: a retrospective genomic epidemiology study.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina.
analysis.methods: Retrospective genomic epidemiology study of 58 human clinical samples collected 2011‑2024 in Chile; samples were sequenced for S, M, and L segments and analysed by phylogenetic inference and variant calling, with clinical and epidemiological data obtained from diagnostic laboratories and surveillance programmes.
analysis.results: Two major genomic variants were identified: ANDV Chi‑North (central Chile) and ANDV‑South (southern Chile); distinct geographic distributions were observed, but no consistent clustering by clinical severity or recurrent non‑synonymous substitutions linked to severe disease; substitutions previously associated with person‑to‑person transmission in Argentina were not consistently seen in Chilean sequences.
analysis.contribution: This is the first nationwide genomic characterisation of human‑derived ANDV in Chile, showing that regional diversification drives viral diversity and that viral genetics alone are unlikely to explain disease severity or person‑to‑person transmission, highlighting the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina. Results: Two major genomic variants were identified: ANDV Chi‑North (central Chile) and ANDV‑South (southern Chile); distinct geographic distributions were observed, but no consistent clustering by clinical severity or recurrent non‑synonymous substitutions linked to severe disease; substitutions previously associated with person‑to‑person transmission in Argentina were not consistently seen in Chilean sequences. Contribution: This is the first nationwide genomic characterisation of human‑derived ANDV in Chile, showing that regional diversification drives viral diversity and that viral genetics alone are unlikely to explain disease severity or person‑to‑person transmission, highlighting the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.

analysis.background: HFRS, caused by hantaviruses, is associated with variable coagulation abnormalities and bleeding. No prior systematic review has synthesized evidence on haemostatic dysfunction in HFRS.
analysis.main_directions: The review characterized thrombocytopenia severity across hantavirus strains (HTNV, DOBV, SEOV vs. PUUV) and coagulation markers like aPTT and D-dimer.
analysis.current_state: Thrombocytopenia is the most consistent finding, with HTNV/DOBV/SEOV causing more severe abnormalities than PUUV. Other parameters show variable reporting.
analysis.gaps: Study quality is inconsistent, and pediatric data are limited. Heterogeneous reporting prevents meta-analysis and robust conclusions.
analysis.future_research: Standardized prospective studies are needed to quantify coagulation changes and bleeding risks, particularly in children and across hantavirus strains.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses shows variable coagulation issues. Main directions: Review compared thrombocytopenia severity across hantavirus strains. Current state: Thrombocytopenia is consistent, but other markers vary. Gaps: Inconsistent study quality and limited pediatric data. Future research: Standardized prospective studies needed.

title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression

analysis.background: Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) cases in Argentina doubled to 101 in 2025–2026, with a geographic shift from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and NOA regions.
analysis.methods: Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression and causal mediation analysis were applied to HPS case data (2020–2026) and climate data (ERA5, NOAA) to assess climate-HPS correlations.
analysis.results: SPI (RR=1.68) and TA (RR=1.45) showed significant positive effects on HPS onset with a 3–4 month lag; spatial analysis linked Buenos Aires outbreaks to flooding and NOA outbreaks to 'drought-flood' extremes.
analysis.contribution: This study provides the first national-scale quantitative causal inference of climate-HPS dynamics in Argentina, identifying vegetation as a key mediator and supporting early warning system development.
analysis.limitations: Evidence is limited to the abstract; detailed methods, sample size, host species, and ecological validation are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Argentina's HPS cases doubled in 2025–2026 with geographic shifts. Methods: Bayesian spatiotemporal models and causal mediation analysis linked climate data to HPS cases. Results: SPI and temperature anomalies correlated with HPS onset, with regional climate patterns driving outbreaks. Contribution: First national-scale climate-HPS causal analysis supports early warning systems. Limitations: Abstract-only evidence lacks detailed methods and ecological validation.

title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change is reshaping biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover at human-wildlife interfaces. The 2026 multi-country cruise ship outbreak of Andes virus highlights the globalized threat of rodent-borne orthohantaviruses, which cause severe, fatal human diseases. Macroecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to hantavirus hazards remains lacking.
analysis.methods: An integrated ecological and machine learning framework combined 89 virus-host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force-of-Infection (FOI) modeling. Projections were generated under climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) for 2041–2060.
analysis.results: Models predict a spatially uneven but extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with 74.9% of modeled land area experiencing increased FOI. Three high-hazard belts were identified in temperate regions. Transmission dynamics vary by virus: thermal/precipitation anomalies drive Old/New World lineages, while urban-centric profiles emerge for Seoul and Mamore viruses.
analysis.contribution: The study provides a baseline for global surveillance and underscores the need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness. It reveals climate-driven restructuring of spillover risks across diverse environments.
analysis.limitations: The paper does not report sample sizes, host population details, or validation metrics. Findings are based on projections under climate scenarios, which may not account for all ecological complexities.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Climate change accelerates hantavirus spillover risks, exemplified by the 2026 Andes virus outbreak. Methods: FOI modeling combined 89 virus-host associations with climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5) for 2041–2060. Results: 74.9% of land areas face increased FOI, with three high-hazard belts. Transmission varies by virus ecology. Contribution: Maps guide surveillance and highlight conservation-public health integration. Limitations: Sample sizes, host details, and validation metrics are unreported.

title: Neurological Manifestations of Hantavirus Infection: A Review.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks with high mortality and occasional person‑to‑person spread, yet their neurological complications remain poorly understood (A1‑A3).
analysis.main_directions: Two syndromes—pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome—are linked to neurological injury, often secondary to hypoxia or metabolic dysfunction, and can manifest as meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, or peripheral neuropathy (A4‑A9).
analysis.current_state: No approved antivirals exist; management is supportive, though some preclinical agents show promise, and neurological manifestations are uncommon but potentially severe (A10‑A12).
analysis.gaps: Prospective studies and experimental models are lacking to delineate clinical features, pathophysiology, therapeutic targets, and management guidelines (A13).
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on prospective cohort studies, animal and cellular models, mechanistic investigations, and the development of evidence‑based treatment protocols (A13).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks with high mortality and occasional person‑to‑person spread, yet their neurological complications remain poorly understood. Main directions: Two syndromes—pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome—are linked to neurological injury, often secondary to hypoxia or metabolic dysfunction, and can manifest as meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, seizures, myelitis, or peripheral neuropathy. Current state: No approved antivirals exist; management is supportive, though some preclinical agents show promise, and neurological manifestations are uncommon but potentially severe. Gaps: Prospective studies and experimental models are lacking to delineate clinical features, pathophysiology, therapeutic targets, and management guidelines. Future research: Future work should focus on prospective cohort studies, animal and cellular models, mechanistic investigations, and the development of evidence‑based treatment protocols.
