title: HantaWatch: Federated Learning for Hantavirus Genomic Surveillance

analysis.background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is constrained by distributed data, non‑IID heterogeneity, and limited expert review capacity.
analysis.main_directions: HantaWatch introduces federated learning for collaborative model training, integrating k‑mer extraction, source‑aware client construction, adaptive DU‑FedProx optimization, surveillance‑specific model selection, and prediction‑only triage.
analysis.current_state: Experiments demonstrate that HantaWatch enables high‑risk screening, outbreak prediction, clade classification, and clinical syndrome categorization while balancing performance, false‑negative risk, and update stability.
analysis.gaps: Current limitations include reliance on distributed sequence data and the need for expert‑review capacity to interpret model outputs.
analysis.future_research: Future work should refine risk‑score conversion, uncertainty flagging, and expert‑review prioritization to enhance decision‑support and evaluate real‑world deployment.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is constrained by distributed data, non‑IID heterogeneity, and limited expert review capacity. title: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.

analysis.background: Orthohantaviruses pose a zoonotic threat in Southern South America (SSA) with 13 rodent reservoir species hosting 15 viral genotypes. Scattered distribution data limit regional risk assessments.
analysis.methods: Reservoir host occurrence records were compiled and ecological niche modeling estimated potential distributions. Binary presence predictions were integrated to map reservoir/genotype richness and human exposure using population data.
analysis.results: Reservoirs are concentrated in eastern Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil. Hotspots of reservoir richness occur in the Humid Chaco, while genotype richness extends across the Chaco-Pampean plain. ≥7 genotypes may expose >13 million people.
analysis.contribution: First region-wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions in SSA, providing ecological baselines for surveillance and prevention strategies.
analysis.limitations: Abstract-only evidence; specific study design, sample size, and host sampling methods not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Orthohantaviruses threaten SSA with 13 rodent reservoirs hosting 15 genotypes. Methods: Ecological niche modeling mapped reservoir distributions and genotype richness using occurrence records and population data. Results: Reservoirs cluster in eastern Argentina/Paraguay/Brazil; 13M+ people may face ≥7 genotypes. Contribution: First region-wide baseline for surveillance. Limitations: Abstract-only evidence.

title: IMMUNOINFORMATICS-GUIDED MULTI-EPITOPE VACCINE CANDIDATE DESIGN AND MHC DOCKING VALIDATION TARGETING THE GLYCOPROTEIN PRECURSOR OF THE NEWLY REPORTED ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 ORTHOHANTAVIRUS

analysis.background: The study addresses the need for rapid vaccine design against newly emerging orthohantaviruses, leveraging genomic data before experimental antigen characterization.
analysis.methods: The glycoprotein precursor (GP) of ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 was analyzed using antigenicity prediction (VaxiJen), conserved-region screening, MHC-I/II epitope prediction, and molecular docking against MHC receptors.
analysis.results: The GP showed antigenicity (VaxiJen score 0.5194). Two CTL and six HTL epitopes were selected. Four multi-epitope constructs (F1-F4) exhibited strong MHC docking, with F4 (MHC-I: -226.92) and F2 (MHC-II: -260.14) as top candidates.
analysis.contribution: This in silico workflow identifies prioritized vaccine candidates for experimental validation, supporting early-stage vaccine development against a newly reported orthohantavirus.
analysis.limitations: The analysis is based solely on the abstract; experimental validation, population coverage, and allergenicity/toxicity data are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Rapid vaccine design for emerging orthohantaviruses is critical. Methods: GP of ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 was analyzed via immunoinformatics and MHC docking. Results: Two CTL, six HTL epitopes, and four constructs (F1-F4) were identified, with F4 (MHC-I: -226.92) and F2 (MHC-II: -260.14) as top candidates. Contribution: Prioritizes vaccine candidates for experimental validation. Limitations: Abstract-only analysis; lacks experimental data.

title: Hantavirus infections in a changing world: epidemiology, pathogenesis, and one health challenges.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics.
analysis.main_directions: Transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, hantaviruses cause haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS), with emerging urban-associated strains such as Seoul virus (SEOV) linked to brown rats and pet rodents.
analysis.current_state: Recent environmental changes, increased human–animal interaction, and global mobility have altered epidemiological patterns, yet the core transmission routes and clinical syndromes remain well defined.
analysis.gaps: Challenges persist in vaccine development, surveillance, and integrating One Health strategies, limiting early detection and control of hantavirus outbreaks.
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on multidisciplinary surveillance, vaccine research, and strengthening early detection systems to mitigate the global impact of hantavirus infections.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics. Current state: Recent environmental changes, increased human–animal interaction, and global mobility have altered epidemiological patterns, yet the core transmission routes and clinical syndromes remain well defined. Gaps: Challenges persist in vaccine development, surveillance, and integrating One Health strategies, limiting early detection and control of hantavirus outbreaks. Future research: Future work should focus on multidisciplinary surveillance, vaccine research, and strengthening early detection systems to mitigate the global impact of hantavirus infections.

title: Hantavirus evidence in West and Central Asia: a scoping review protocol with implications for surveillance

analysis.background: Hantaviruses, hosted by rodents and small mammals, cause diverse human diseases, but regional surveillance in West and Central Asia remains fragmented.
analysis.main_directions: The review focuses on ecological, diagnostic, and surveillance factors shaping hantavirus dynamics, with evidence scattered across clinical, serological, and molecular studies.
analysis.current_state: Hantaviruses are confirmed in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, but evidence quality and availability vary significantly between regions.
analysis.gaps: Limited molecular confirmation, reliance on single serological assays, and sparse data in some countries hinder comprehensive understanding.
analysis.future_research: Integrated human-animal surveillance, standardized molecular diagnostics, and targeted studies in underreported regions are critical for robust surveillance.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses hosted by rodents cause diverse human diseases, but regional surveillance in West/Central Asia is fragmented. Main directions: Review focuses on ecological, diagnostic, and surveillance factors with evidence from clinical, serological, and molecular studies. Current state: Confirmed in Turkey, Iran, Kazakhstan, and the South Caucasus, but evidence quality varies. Gaps: Limited molecular confirmation and sparse data in some countries. Future research: Integrated surveillance, standardized diagnostics, and studies in underreported regions.

title: ANALYSIS OF GENOTYPE FREQUENCIES OF MONONUCLEOTIDE POLYMORPHISMS OF EARLY IMMUNE RESPONSE GENES IN HFRS PATIENTS

analysis.background: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is a clinically variable disease that remains a major focal infection in Russia.
analysis.methods: The study genotyped single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the CASP1 (rs580253) and IL‑6 (rs1800795) genes in HFRS patients and a control group, determining allele and genotype frequencies.
analysis.results: In HFRS patients, genotype frequencies deviated significantly from Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, suggesting a correlation between these SNPs and disease manifestations.
analysis.contribution: The findings indicate that early immune response gene polymorphisms may influence HFRS clinical variability, providing a basis for further genetic surveillance and risk stratification.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: A 5-Adic Ultrametric Framework for Alignment-Free Phylogenetic Analysis of Hantavirus RNA Sequences

analysis.background: The study addresses the need for a non-Archimedean framework to represent and analyze genomic sequences, particularly for alignment‑free phylogenetic inference of Hantavirus RNA, a problem of importance for evolutionary biology.
analysis.methods: The authors embed RNA sequences into a compact ultrametric space via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic mapping, prove it is a global isometry, and develop a continuous‑time evolutionary model using a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential operator, including finite‑resolution projections onto quotient rings of ℕ_5.
analysis.results: They demonstrate that the embedding yields a compact Cantor‑type subset of ℕ_5, derive a non‑Archimedean diffusion equation with an explicit fundamental solution via 5‑adic Fourier analysis, and construct an ultrametric distance function that captures hierarchical relationships through shared symbolic prefixes.
analysis.contribution: The work bridges p‑adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, pseudo‑differential operators, and computational phylogenetics, offering a mathematically rigorous, alignment‑free framework illustrated on Hantavirus genomes.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study addresses the need for a non‑Archimedean framework to represent and analyze genomic sequences, particularly for alignment‑free phylogenetic inference of Hantavirus RNA, a problem of importance for evolutionary biology. Methods: The authors embed RNA sequences into a compact ultrametric space via an injective symbolic‑to‑arithmetic mapping, prove it is a global isometry, and develop a continuous‑time evolutionary model using a Vladimirov pseudo‑differential operator, including finite‑resolution projections onto quotient rings of Z5. Results: They demonstrate that the embedding yields a compact Cantor‑type subset of Z5, derive a non‑Archimedean diffusion equation with an explicit fundamental solution via 5‑adic Fourier analysis, and construct an ultrametric distance function that captures hierarchical relationships through shared symbolic prefixes. Contribution: The work bridges p‑adic analysis, ultrametric geometry, pseudo‑differential operators, and computational phylogenetics, offering a mathematically rigorous, alignment‑free framework illustrated on Hantavirus genomes. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Serologic Evidence of Hantavirus Exposure in Humans, Italy, 2004-2018.

analysis.background: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed.
analysis.methods: Serum samples from 371 patients (2004–2018) in northwestern Italy were tested for hantavirus and LCMV IgG using immunofluorescence assay (IFA) and focus reduction neutralization tests (FRNT).
analysis.results: Hantavirus IgG was detected in 7.3% (95% Wilson CI 5.0–10.4%) of samples, with PUUV-neutralizing antibodies confirmed in 10/11 cases (titers 1:80–>1:320); LCMV IgG was detected in 2.7% (95% Wilson CI 1.5–4.9%).
analysis.contribution: This study provides evidence of unrecognized local hantavirus circulation in northwestern Italy, with higher seroprevalence than previously reported in the region.
analysis.limitations: The study population included patients with chronic diseases, potentially introducing selection bias; clinical data on symptoms or exposure were not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Rodentborne hantaviruses and LCMV circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed. Contribution: This study provides evidence of unrecognized local hantavirus circulation in northwestern Italy, with higher seroprevalence than previously reported in the region. Artificial intelligence for chest imaging in hantavirus pulmonary syndrome: A review of practical considerations for rare-disease imaging.

analysis.background: HPS is a rare zoonotic respiratory disease with high fatality, and recent travel-associated Andes virus clusters have heightened the need for early radiographic recognition outside endemic areas.
analysis.main_directions: The review highlights that AI tools developed during COVID‑19 could inform HPS chest imaging, but application remains largely unexplored; it outlines imaging features, lessons from COVID‑19 AI, and practical development considerations.
analysis.current_state: Imaging features of HPS are known across modalities, and AI experience from COVID‑19 provides a framework, yet no disease‑specific AI models exist for HPS.
analysis.gaps: Data scarcity, low pretest probability in non‑endemic settings, overlapping imaging findings, and translational pitfalls limit AI development for HPS.
analysis.future_research: Multi‑institutional datasets, prevalence‑aware evaluation, and multimodal workflow integration combining imaging with clinical, laboratory, and epidemiologic data are needed.

title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is a hantavirus capable of human-to-human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome.
analysis.main_directions: The review summarizes epidemiology, clinical features, transmission routes, and public health responses using the 2026 MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak as a case study.
analysis.current_state: By May 27, 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths were reported; genomic data showed minimal variation, indicating onboard human-to-human spread with an Rₑ of 0.7, driven by age, shared cabins, and close contact.
analysis.gaps: Early detection was delayed, and the small outbreak size limits generalizability of transmission dynamics and control measures.
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on routine travel history assessment, enhanced surveillance for suspected cases, rapid diagnostics, and preparedness for importation of low‑transmissibility pathogens in high‑density settings.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) is a hantavirus capable of human-to-human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome. Main directions: The review summarizes epidemiology, clinical features, transmission routes, and public health responses using the 2026 MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak as a case study. Current state: By May 27, 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths were reported; genomic data showed minimal variation, indicating onboard human-to-human spread with an Rₑ of 0.7, driven by age, shared cabins, and close contact. Gaps: Early detection was delayed, and the small outbreak size limits generalizability of transmission dynamics and control measures. Future research: Future work should focus on routine travel history assessment, enhanced surveillance for suspected cases, rapid diagnostics, and preparedness for importation of low‑transmissibility pathogens in high‑density settings.

title: Spectrum of Gastrointestinal, Hepatobiliary, and Pancreatic Involvement in Hantavirus Infection: A Narrative Review.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infection is a rodent‑borne zoonosis causing hemorrhagic fever with renal failure and pulmonary syndrome, and a recent cruise‑ship outbreak has renewed focus on its clinical spectrum.
analysis.main_directions: GI, hepatobiliary, and pancreatic manifestations—early prodromal GI symptoms, transaminitis, gallbladder changes, pancreatitis—are frequent but under‑studied; supportive care remains mainstay, antivirals show limited benefit.
analysis.current_state: Evidence suggests early GI and liver enzyme elevations may predict severe disease, yet data are mainly case reports and small series.
analysis.gaps: Sparse, heterogeneous data limit understanding of incidence, mechanisms, and prognostic value; lack of prospective studies.
analysis.future_research: Large prospective cohorts and mechanistic studies are needed to quantify prevalence, clarify pathophysiology, and evaluate targeted therapies.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infection, a rodent‑borne zoonosis, causes hemorrhagic fever with renal failure and pulmonary syndrome; a recent cruise‑ship outbreak has highlighted its clinical spectrum. Main directions: GI, hepatobiliary, and pancreatic involvement—early prodromal GI symptoms, transaminitis, gallbladder changes, pancreatitis—are frequent but under‑studied; supportive care remains mainstay, antivirals show limited benefit. Current state: Evidence suggests early GI and liver enzyme elevations may predict severe disease, yet data are mainly case reports and small series. Gaps: Sparse, heterogeneous data limit understanding of incidence, mechanisms, and prognostic value; lack of prospective studies. Future research: Large prospective cohorts and mechanistic studies are needed to quantify prevalence, clarify pathophysiology, and evaluate targeted therapies.

title: Hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in children: a systematic scoping review of presentation, critical care management, and outcomes.

analysis.background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly to severe disease, prompting a need to synthesize limited pediatric data (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review maps pediatric clinical presentation and critical‑care interventions such as mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO and renal replacement therapy (A9).
analysis.current_state: Mortality in reported pediatric series is about 33‑37%, but the evidence is mainly case‑based with variable reporting completeness (A11, A12).
analysis.gaps: Pediatric‑specific evidence is scarce and geographically limited, and reporting completeness is inconsistent, hindering robust conclusions (A2, A12).
analysis.future_research: Prospective multicenter surveillance, standardized case reporting in endemic areas, and studies on optimal timing of advanced cardiopulmonary support are needed (A2, A12).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can rapidly become fatal, necessitating synthesis of limited data. Main directions: The review catalogs pediatric presentation, critical‑care measures (ventilation, vasoactive drugs, ECMO, renal replacement) and outcomes. Current state: Reported mortality is ~33‑37%, yet evidence is largely case‑based with variable completeness. Gaps: Pediatric‑specific data are scarce, geographically concentrated, and inconsistently reported. Future research: Prospective multicenter surveillance, standardized reporting, and trials on optimal timing of advanced cardiopulmonary support are required.

title: Can hantaviruses cause pandemics?

analysis.background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns about the pandemic potential of hantaviruses because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human‑to‑human transmission.
analysis.methods: This article is a narrative review; specific study design, sample collection, or analytical methods are not reported in the supplied evidence.
analysis.results: The outbreak resulted in 11 laboratory‑confirmed ANDV cases and three deaths (≈27% case‑fatality rate) and shows that human‑to‑human transmission can occur under close‑contact conditions; hantaviruses share pandemic‑associated features such as zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences.
analysis.contribution: The review synthesizes current knowledge to highlight characteristics that could allow hantaviruses to cause a pandemic, aiding surveillance and public‑health preparedness.
analysis.limitations: Only a single outbreak is described and no original data are presented; the available material is limited to an abstract and partial text.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus raised concerns about hantavirus pandemic potential because ANDV uniquely transmits between humans. Methods: This is a narrative review; specific methods are not reported. Results: The outbreak yielded 11 confirmed cases and three deaths (≈27% CFR) demonstrating possible human‑to‑human spread, and hantaviruses share pandemic‑linked traits like zoonotic origin and RNA variability. Contribution: The review highlights features that could enable hantaviruses to cause a pandemic, informing surveillance and preparedness. Limitations: Only one outbreak is examined, no original data are provided, and only abstract/partial text are available.

title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact.
analysis.main_directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta and occasional person‑to‑person spread, molecular virology and immune‑mediated pathogenesis, the lack of licensed vaccines and limited antivirals, diagnostic challenges in co‑circulating species, and the influence of environmental change on spillover.
analysis.current_state: Severity of disease varies with viral species, host immune response, and timely care, and current evidence attributes major clinical damage to immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic effects; large‑scale ecological studies have clarified reservoir distribution and environmental determinants.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include the absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited efficacy of antiviral therapies, difficulties in species‑specific diagnostics, and uncertainties about how urbanization and rodent dynamics will affect future spread.
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on vaccine development, antiviral trials, improved species‑level diagnostics, longitudinal ecological surveillance, and mechanistic studies of immunopathogenesis to guide prevention and control.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing HFRS in Eurasia and HPS in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact. Main directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta and occasional person‑to‑person spread, molecular virology and immune‑mediated pathogenesis, the lack of licensed vaccines and limited antivirals, diagnostic challenges in co‑circulating species, and the influence of environmental change on spillover. Current state: Severity of disease varies with viral species, host immune response, and timely care, and current evidence attributes major clinical damage to immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic effects; large‑scale ecological studies have clarified reservoir distribution and environmental determinants. Gaps: Key gaps include the absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited efficacy of antiviral therapies, difficulties in species‑specific diagnostics, and uncertainties about how urbanization and rodent dynamics will affect future spread. Future research: Future work should focus on vaccine development, antiviral trials, improved species‑level diagnostics, longitudinal ecological surveillance, and mechanistic studies of immunopathogenesis to guide prevention and control.

title: Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak

analysis.background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public‑health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal.
analysis.methods: This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition‑era travel medicine.
analysis.results: The outbreak exposed expedition‑specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post‑disembarkation monitoring.
analysis.contribution: Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship‑event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; the authors conclude that expedition cruises require binding, auditable, expedition‑specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available; detailed methods, quantitative results, sample size, location, host information, and other specifics are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius expedition cruise shows how land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public‑health event via shipboard factors. Methods: The paper is a Perspective that analyses this outbreak as a case study of governance and operational gaps. Results: Key gaps identified include safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard febrile‑illness recognition, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post‑disembarkation monitoring. Contribution: Existing IHR, WHO, and ECDC guidance form a coordination base, but the authors argue expedition cruises need binding, auditable, expedition‑specific outbreak protocols. Limitations: Only the abstract is provided; detailed data and methods are not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because it can spread person‑to‑person, and its high case‑fatality rate raises the question of post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
analysis.main_directions: The viewpoint discusses the relevance of ribavirin PEP after high‑risk household, healthcare‑associated, or laboratory exposures and presents modelling of possible dosing strategies.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, but clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV PEP remains extremely limited.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include the absence of controlled human studies, uncertainty about optimal dose and duration, and concerns about ribavirin toxicity.
analysis.future_research: Future work should include controlled clinical trials, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic studies, and safety monitoring to define ribavirin’s efficacy and safe regimens for ANDV PEP.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus can transmit person-to-person and has a high fatality rate, prompting interest in PEP. Main directions: The article reviews ribavirin PEP relevance after high‑risk exposures and models dosing strategies. Current state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal efficacy, but human clinical data for ANDV PEP are scarce. Gaps: No controlled human trials, unclear optimal dosing, and potential toxicity limit use. Future research: Conduct controlled trials, PK/PD studies, and safety monitoring to establish efficacy and safe regimens.

title: Modeling the Transmission Dynamics of Hantavirus with Environmental Viral Persistence

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure occurring mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles (A1).
analysis.methods: The authors formulate and analyse a deterministic compartmental model that includes susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir, incorporating logistic rodent growth, direct rodent‑to‑rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding and natural viral clearance (A2; A3).
analysis.results: The disease‑free equilibrium and the basic reproduction number (R0) are derived (A5); stability analysis shows the disease‑free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1 and an endemic equilibrium exists when R0 > 1 (A6); the analysis indicates that environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway sustaining transmission among rodents and humans (A8).
analysis.contribution: The model provides a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions such as seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data‑driven parameterisation (A10).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided, so detailed parameter values, empirical validation, and real‑world data are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis transmitted to humans via contaminated environments (A1). Methods: A deterministic compartmental model linking rodents, humans and an environmental viral reservoir was constructed, including logistic rodent growth, direct and indirect transmission, shedding and clearance (A2; A3). Results: The basic reproduction number (R0) and disease‑free equilibrium were derived; R0 < 1 yields a stable disease‑free state, while R0 > 1 permits an endemic equilibrium, and environmental persistence can sustain transmission (A5; A6; A8). Contribution: The framework offers a tractable tool for understanding hantavirus persistence and informs future model extensions (A10). Limitations: Only an abstract is available, lacking detailed data, validation, and parameter estimates.

title: Hantavirus and Middle East Public Health Challenges: A Subject Review

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses in rodent reservoirs transmitted to humans via saliva, aerosols, or excreta (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review outlines geographic distribution, environmental drivers, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostics, and public‑health preparedness in the Middle East (A6).
analysis.current_state: Recent studies show low but detectable hantavirus circulation in countries such as Türkiye and Iran (A3), and while human cases are rare, ecological conditions that could sustain transmission are present (A7).
analysis.gaps: Surveillance is limited, cases are underdiagnosed, and clinical presentation overlaps with other febrile illnesses, hindering accurate burden estimation (A5).
analysis.future_research: Enhanced surveillance, interdisciplinary One‑Health initiatives, and stronger collaboration among medical, veterinary, and environmental sectors are recommended (A7, A8).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonoses transmitted from rodents to humans (A1). Main directions: The review covers distribution, ecology, hosts, epidemiology, clinical aspects, diagnostics, and preparedness in the Middle East (A6). Current state: Low but confirmed circulation in Türkiye, Iran and others, with rare human cases yet suitable ecological conditions (A3, A7). Gaps: Limited surveillance, underdiagnosis, and symptom overlap impede burden assessment (A5). Future research: Expand surveillance, adopt One‑Health approaches, and foster medical‑veterinary‑environmental collaboration (A7, A8).

title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change is reshuffling global biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover at the human‑wildlife interface.
analysis.methods: The study combined 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force‑of‑Infection (FOI) modeling under SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5 climate scenarios for 2041–2060.
analysis.results: Models predict a spatially uneven but extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with ~74.9% of modeled land area showing increased FOI, and identify three high‑hazard belts in temperate reservoir ranges.
analysis.contribution: The work provides a global baseline map for targeted surveillance and underscores the need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Anthropogenic climate change is reshuffling global biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover at the human‑wildlife interface. Methods: The study combined 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force‑of‑Infection modeling under SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5 climate scenarios for 2041–2060. Results: Models predict a spatially uneven but extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with ~74.9% of modeled land area showing increased FOI, and identify three high‑hazard belts in temperate reservoir ranges. Contribution: The work provides a global baseline map for targeted surveillance and underscores the need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: First nationwide full-genome characterisation of human-derived Andes virus in Chile: a retrospective genomic epidemiology study.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina; its genomic diversity in Chile is incompletely characterised.
analysis.methods: A retrospective genomic epidemiology study analysed clinical samples from patients with confirmed ANDV collected between 9 Mar 2011 and 27 Jun 2024, with clinical and epidemiological data from diagnostic labs and surveillance programmes; consensus sequences for S, M, and L segments were generated and phylogenetic inference and variant calling were used to assess genetic clustering and divergence.
analysis.results: From 58 infected individuals, two major genomic variants with distinct geographical distributions (ANDV Chi‑North in central Chile and ANDV‑South in southern Chile) were identified; no consistent clustering by clinical severity or recurrent non‑synonymous substitutions uniquely linked to severe disease were observed, and substitutions previously associated with person‑to‑person transmission were not consistently present, even in four transmission cases.
analysis.contribution: The study provides the first nationwide genomic characterisation of human‑derived ANDV in Chile, showing regional diversification rather than clinical outcome drives viral diversity and indicating that disease severity or person‑to‑person transmission are unlikely explained solely by viral genetics, thereby informing future integrated genomic surveillance.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available, limiting detail on sample selection, sequencing depth, statistical analyses, and broader contextual information.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and causes severe disease in Chile and Argentina, yet its Chilean genomic diversity is poorly known. Methods: Researchers retrospectively sequenced S, M, and L segments from 58 confirmed cases collected 2011‑2024, using phylogenetic and variant analyses. Results: Two geographically distinct variants (Chi‑North and South) were found; no viral signatures consistently linked to severity or person‑to‑person spread. Contribution: This first nationwide human‑derived ANDV genomic map highlights regional diversification and suggests genetics alone do not explain clinical outcomes, supporting enhanced surveillance. Limitations: Only abstract data are provided, restricting methodological and analytical detail.

title: Andes Virus - A Clinical Review.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only orthohantavirus with documented human‑to‑human transmission (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review summarizes ANDV epidemiology, clinical presentation, and best‑practice management based on expert consensus, field experience, and trial data (A2).
analysis.current_state: It evaluates currently available and investigational treatments, including antivirals, monoclonal antibodies, and outlines vaccine development prospects (A3).
analysis.gaps: The abstract does not specify unresolved questions, evidence weaknesses, or methodological limitations, highlighting a gap in explicit identification of uncertainties (A4).
analysis.future_research: Further clinical trials of antivirals and monoclonal antibodies, development and testing of vaccines, and implementation studies of infection‑prevention measures are needed (A3).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus is the sole orthohantavirus with proven human‑to‑human spread (A1). Main directions: The article reviews epidemiology, clinical features, and management guidelines drawn from consensus, field work, and trials (A2). Current state: It assesses existing and experimental antivirals, monoclonal antibodies, and vaccine prospects (A3). Gaps: The abstract lacks explicit discussion of knowledge gaps or methodological limits (A4). Future research: Calls for more trials of antivirals, monoclonal antibodies, vaccine development, and studies on infection‑control implementation (A3).

title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.

analysis.background: Haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is caused by hantaviruses and is associated with variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding manifestations.
analysis.main_directions: The review aimed to characterise the clinical and laboratory features of coagulation disturbances in HFRS to inform disease mechanisms and future research.
analysis.current_state: Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent abnormality, more pronounced in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections compared with PUUV, which is associated with milder disease; mild prolongation of aPTT and elevated D‑dimer were variably reported.
analysis.gaps: Variable study quality and incomplete reporting limit firm conclusions, underscoring the need for standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes.
analysis.future_research: Future work should involve standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes to clarify mechanisms and guide patient care.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is caused by hantaviruses and is associated with variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding manifestations. Main directions: The review aimed to characterise the clinical and laboratory features of coagulation disturbances in HFRS to inform disease mechanisms and future research. Current state: Thrombocytopenia was the most consistent abnormality, more pronounced in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV infections compared with PUUV, which is associated with milder disease; mild prolongation of aPTT and elevated D‑dimer were variably reported. Gaps: Variable study quality and incomplete reporting limit firm conclusions, underscoring the need for standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes. Future research: Future work should involve standardised, prospective studies of coagulation and bleeding outcomes to clarify mechanisms and guide patient care.

title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression

analysis.background: Objective: The number of confirmed Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) cases in Argentina surged to 101 in the 2025–2026 season, nearly doubling the previous season, with a geographic shift from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region.
analysis.methods: Confirmed HPS cases (2020‑2026) with coordinates were compiled; ERA5‑Land and NOAA GSOD data were accessed via Google Earth Engine to compute monthly temperature anomalies, SPI, and soil‑moisture anomalies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression with INLA estimated 1‑6 month lag effects and spatial heterogeneity; causal mediation analysis examined climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human pathways.
analysis.results: Spatial instability was indicated (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001). SPI (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI 1.31–2.14) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature anomaly (RR = 1.45 per 1 °C, 95% CrI 1.12–1.87) positively affected HPS, with synergistic interaction (RERI = 0.79). Flood months raised Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold; drought‑flood events drove NOA outbreaks. NDVI mediated 41.2% (Buenos Aires) and 56.7% (NOA) of the climate effect.
analysis.contribution: First national‑scale quantitative causal inference linking climate anomalies to hantavirus outbreaks in Argentina, confirming a drought‑flood‑rodent‑human pathway and providing a GEE‑based framework for a climate‑driven early warning system.
analysis.limitations: Only the abstract is available; details on case verification, model validation, and potential confounders are not reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HPS cases surged to 101 in 2025‑2026, nearly doubling the prior season and shifting eastward and northwestward. Methods: Cases (2020‑2026) with coordinates were linked to ERA5‑Land and NOAA climate data via GEE; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression (INLA) assessed lagged climate effects; causal mediation analysis explored climate‑vegetation‑rodent‑human chains. Results: Spatial instability (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68) at 3‑4 month lag and temperature anomaly (RR = 1.45 per °C) increased risk, with interaction (RERI = 0.79); flood events raised Buenos Aires risk 2.3‑fold; drought‑flood events linked to NOA outbreaks; NDVI mediated 41.2%–56.7% of climate impact. Contribution: Provides the first national causal quantification of climate‑driven hantavirus risk and a GEE‑based early warning framework. Limitations: Only abstract data are available, lacking detailed validation and confounder assessment.

title: Neurological Manifestations of Hantavirus Infection: A Review.

analysis.background: Hantaviruses have caused several recent human disease outbreaks and carry a high mortality rate, prompting a review of their neurological impact (A1, A2).
analysis.main_directions: The review outlines two major clinical syndromes—pulmonary and hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome—and describes neurological involvement that is often secondary to hypoxic/ischemic injury, as well as meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, and rare seizures, myelitis, or peripheral neuropathy (A4, A5, A6, A8, A9).
analysis.current_state: Neurological complications of hantavirus infection are documented but remain poorly understood, typically arising from secondary metabolic dysfunction, and although uncommon, they can be severe (A3, A6, A12).
analysis.gaps: There are no approved specific antivirals, the pathophysiology of direct neuroinvasion is unclear, and evidence is limited to case observations without systematic study (A10, A13).
analysis.future_research: Prospective clinical studies and experimental animal models are needed to delineate neurological manifestations, clarify mechanisms, identify therapeutic targets, and develop management guidelines (A13).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses have caused recent outbreaks with high mortality, motivating a review of neuro effects (A1, A2). Main directions: Two syndromes (pulmonary, hemorrhagic fever with renal) are linked to secondary hypoxic injury, meningoencephalitis, pituitary apoplexy, and rare seizures, myelitis, neuropathy (A4, A5, A6, A8, A9). Current state: Neurological complications are documented but poorly understood and can be severe (A3, A6, A12). Gaps: No specific antivirals, unclear direct neuro‑pathogenesis, and lack of systematic data (A10, A13). Future research: Prospective studies and experimental models to characterize manifestations, mechanisms, therapeutic targets, and guidelines (A13).

title: HantaWatch: Federated Learning for Hantavirus Genomic Surveillance

analysis.background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is constrained by distributed sequence data, non‑IID source heterogeneity, and limited expert‑review capacity.
analysis.main_directions: HantaWatch introduces a federated learning framework that allows laboratories to collaboratively train sequence‑based models without sharing raw data, employing k‑mer feature extraction, source‑aware client construction, adaptive DU‑FedProx optimization, surveillance‑specific model selection, and prediction‑only triage.
analysis.current_state: Experimental results demonstrate that HantaWatch supports high‑risk screening, outbreak‑associated prediction, clade classification, and clinical syndrome categorization while balancing predictive performance, false‑negative risk, and update stability.
analysis.gaps: The abstract indicates limited evidence on real‑world deployment, handling of extreme data heterogeneity, model interpretability, and integration with existing public health workflows.
analysis.future_research: Future work should validate HantaWatch in operational settings, quantify its impact on expert workload, refine uncertainty quantification, extend the approach to other zoonotic pathogens, and develop policy guidelines for federated genomic surveillance.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus genomic surveillance is limited by distributed data, heterogeneity, and constrained expert capacity. Main directions: HantaWatch offers a federated learning framework enabling collaborative model training without raw data sharing, using k‑mer features, source‑aware clients, adaptive DU‑FedProx, and surveillance‑specific selection. Current state: Experiments show high‑risk screening, outbreak prediction, clade classification, and syndrome categorization with balanced performance and stability. Gaps: Abstract‑level evidence lacks real‑world deployment data, heterogeneity handling, interpretability, and workflow integration. Future research: Validate in practice, assess expert‑workload impact, improve uncertainty estimates, broaden to other pathogens, and establish policy guidelines.

title: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.

analysis.background: Orthohantaviruses pose a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 native rodent species identified as reservoirs for 15 viral genotypes.
analysis.methods: The study compiled and curated occurrence records for these reservoir hosts and applied ecological niche modeling, integrating binary presence predictions to map reservoir and genotype richness, then combined these maps with gridded human population data.
analysis.results: Reservoir species are mainly distributed across eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; hotspots of reservoir richness lie in the Humid Chaco, while high genotype richness extends across the Chaco‑Pampean plain, with an estimated >13 million people potentially exposed to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes.
analysis.contribution: This work provides the first integrated, region‑wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, establishing an updated ecological baseline to support surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; only the abstract is available, limiting detail on methods, sample sizes, and specific limitations.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Orthohantaviruses pose a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America, with 13 native rodent species identified as reservoirs for 15 viral genotypes. Methods: The study compiled and curated occurrence records for these reservoir hosts and applied ecological niche modeling, integrating binary presence predictions to map reservoir and genotype richness, then combined these maps with gridded human population data. Results: Reservoir species are mainly distributed across eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil; hotspots of reservoir richness lie in the Humid Chaco, while high genotype richness extends across the Chaco‑Pampean plain, with an estimated >13 million people potentially exposed to ≥7 pathogenic genotypes. Contribution: This work provides the first integrated, region‑wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, establishing an updated ecological baseline to support surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; only the abstract is available, limiting detail on methods, sample sizes, and specific limitations.

title: Watching the Ship, Missing the Forest: Why Ecological Spillover, Reservoir Ecology, and Environmental Change Remain the True Hantavirus Threats in North America.
Artificial intelligence for chest imaging in hantavirus pulmonary syndrome: A review of practical considerations for rare-disease imaging.

analysis.background: HPS is a rare, high‑fatality zoonotic respiratory disease, and recent travel‑associated Andes orthohantavirus clusters have highlighted the need for early radiographic recognition outside endemic areas.
analysis.main_directions: The review focuses on imaging features of HPS, lessons from AI applications in COVID‑19 and viral pneumonia, and practical considerations for developing HPS‑aware AI, including dataset creation, labeling, comparator selection, modeling, validation, and workflow integration.
analysis.current_state: AI tools from the COVID‑19 era are now part of radiology workflows, yet their application to HPS chest imaging remains largely unexplored, and current knowledge is limited to narrative synthesis of imaging findings.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include data scarcity, low pretest probability in non‑endemic settings, overlapping imaging findings with other pneumonias, and translational challenges from existing AI literature.
analysis.future_research: Future work should involve multi‑institutional data collection, prevalence‑aware evaluation, and multimodal integration of imaging with clinical, laboratory, and epidemiologic data.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HPS is a rare, high‑fatality zoonotic respiratory disease, and recent travel‑associated Andes orthohantavirus clusters have highlighted the need for early radiographic recognition outside endemic areas. Main directions: The review focuses on imaging features of HPS, lessons from AI applications in COVID‑19 and viral pneumonia, and practical considerations for developing HPS‑aware AI, including dataset creation, labeling, comparator selection, modeling, validation, and workflow integration. Current state: AI tools from the COVID‑19 era are now part of radiology workflows, yet their application to HPS chest imaging remains largely unexplored, and current knowledge is limited to narrative synthesis of imaging findings. Gaps: Key gaps include data scarcity, low pretest probability in non‑endemic settings, overlapping imaging findings with other pneumonias, and translational challenges from existing AI literature. Future research: Future work should involve multi‑institutional data collection, prevalence‑aware evaluation, and multimodal integration of imaging with clinical, laboratory, and epidemiologic data.

title: 2026 Cruise Ship-associated Andes Hantavirus Outbreak and Public Health Response.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is a hantavirus capable of human-to-human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America.
analysis.main_directions: The review summarizes epidemiology, clinical features, and transmission routes of hantavirus infections, focusing on transmission patterns in a confined, high-density environment and public health response strategies, illustrated by the 2026 MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak.
analysis.current_state: In the MV Hondius outbreak, 13 individuals (11 confirmed, 2 probable) were infected and 3 died; genomic analysis showed minimal variation, indicating onboard human-to-human transmission, and the effective reproduction number was 0.7 on May 22.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is limited to publicly available outbreak reports and lacks detailed data on transmission dynamics and risk factors beyond broad observations.
analysis.future_research: Future work should enhance rapid diagnostic testing, routine travel history assessment, surveillance for suspected cases, and coordinated international risk communication to prepare for potential importation of pathogens with limited human-to-human transmissibility.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) is a hantavirus capable of human-to-human transmission and causes severe hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome with high lethality in South America. Main directions: The review summarizes epidemiology, clinical features, and transmission routes of hantavirus infections, focusing on transmission patterns in a confined, high-density environment and public health response strategies, illustrated by the 2026 MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak. Current state: In the MV Hondius outbreak, 13 individuals (11 confirmed, 2 probable) were infected and 3 died; genomic analysis showed minimal variation, indicating onboard human-to-human transmission, and the effective reproduction number was 0.7 on May 22. Gaps: Evidence is limited to publicly available outbreak reports and lacks detailed data on transmission dynamics and risk factors beyond broad observations. Future research: Future work should enhance rapid diagnostic testing, routine travel history assessment, surveillance for suspected cases, and coordinated international risk communication to prepare for potential importation of pathogens with limited human-to-human transmissibility.

title: Spectrum of Gastrointestinal, Hepatobiliary, and Pancreatic Involvement in Hantavirus Infection: A Narrative Review.

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis causing hemorrhagic fever with renal failure or pulmonary syndrome, and a recent cruise‑ship outbreak has highlighted its clinical spectrum.
analysis.main_directions: GI, hepatobiliary, and pancreatic involvement are frequent early prodromes, including nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, GI bleeding, transaminitis, gallbladder wall thickening, cholecystitis, autoimmune hepatitis, and pancreatitis; management is mainly supportive with limited antiviral benefit.
analysis.current_state: The narrative review synthesizes literature from 2000‑2026, noting early GI symptoms precede organ injury and that most data come from case reports and small studies.
analysis.gaps: Evidence is sparse and heterogeneous, with inconsistent links between GI/hepatic findings and severity, and a lack of prospective data limits understanding of mechanisms and prognostic value.
analysis.future_research: Large prospective studies are needed to define incidence, mechanisms, and prognostic significance of GI and hepatopancreatic manifestations in hantavirus infection.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis causing hemorrhagic fever with renal failure or pulmonary syndrome, and a recent cruise‑ship outbreak has highlighted its clinical spectrum. Main directions: GI, hepatobiliary, and pancreatic involvement are frequent early prodromes, including nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, GI bleeding, transaminitis, gallbladder wall thickening, cholecystitis, autoimmune hepatitis, and pancreatitis; management is mainly supportive with limited antiviral benefit. Current state: The narrative review synthesizes literature from 2000‑2026, noting early GI symptoms precede organ injury and that most data come from case reports and small studies. Gaps: Evidence is sparse and heterogeneous, with inconsistent links between GI/hepatic findings and severity, and a lack of prospective data limits understanding of mechanisms and prognostic value. Future research: Large prospective studies are needed to define incidence, mechanisms, and prognostic significance of GI and hepatopancreatic manifestations in hantavirus infection.

title: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice.

analysis.background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease.
analysis.methods: A descriptive analytical cross‑sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025‑2026 academic year, using the researcher‑developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP‑NS); data were analysed with descriptive statistics, Cronbach's alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors.
analysis.results: Mean knowledge score was 8.13/20 (SD 5.84) with 59 % classified as poor; attitude mean was 33.34/50 (SD 7.78) and practice mean 42.98/64 (SD 15.83). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001) while knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). Prior hantavirus teaching, infection‑prevention training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge, and attitude was the strongest independent predictor of practice.
analysis.contribution: The study identifies important disease‑specific knowledge gaps among nursing students and demonstrates that attitude, rather than knowledge, predicts preventive practice, suggesting that scenario‑based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal to improve preparedness for rodent‑borne zoonoses.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; abstract only.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Methods: A descriptive cross‑sectional survey of 236 UAE nursing students used a custom questionnaire and statistical analyses including regression. Results: Knowledge was low (mean 8.13/20, 59% poor); attitude moderate (33.34/50) and practice higher (42.98/64); attitude correlated with practice (r=0.569, p<0.001) while knowledge did not; prior teaching and IPC training predicted knowledge and attitude predicted practice. Contribution: Highlights knowledge gaps and that attitude drives practice, informing One Health‑based education for zoonotic preparedness. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; abstract only.

title: Can hantaviruses cause pandemics?

analysis.background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns about the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human‑to‑human transmission, and the recent COVID‑19 pandemic underscored the impact of emerging pathogens.
analysis.methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence.
analysis.results: Hantaviruses are segmented negative‑sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to humans primarily by inhalation of contaminated aerosols (A3). Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in the Americas and carries high mortality (A4). The MV Hondius outbreak involved 147 passengers/crew, 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, yielding a case‑fatality rate of ~27% (F9‑F10). Although most hantaviruses lack human‑to‑human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close‑contact conditions (A5).
analysis.contribution: The review compares hantaviruses with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens, highlighting shared pandemic‑associated features such as zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences, which informs surveillance and risk assessment (A6).
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; the paper is a narrative review without detailed methodological description, systematic data collection, or quantitative risk modeling.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) raised concerns about hantavirus pandemic potential, as ANDV uniquely shows human‑to‑human transmission and COVID‑19 highlighted such risks. Methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence. Results: Hantaviruses are rodent‑borne, negative‑sense RNA viruses transmitted via aerosols (A3); they cause HFRS in Europe/Asia and HCPS in the Americas with high mortality (A4). The MV Hondius incident involved 147 people, 11 confirmed cases and three deaths (≈27% CFR) (F9‑F10). While most lack human‑to‑human spread, ANDV can transmit between humans under close contact (A5). Contribution: The review identifies pandemic‑related traits of hantaviruses—zoonotic origin, genome variability, environmental factors—supporting surveillance and risk assessment (A6). Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence; the article provides a narrative synthesis without detailed methods or quantitative analysis.

title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact.
analysis.main_directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta, molecular virology and immune‑mediated pathogenesis, limited therapeutic options, diagnostic challenges, and the influence of environmental change on spread.
analysis.current_state: Disease severity varies with viral species, host immune response, and prompt medical care, and immune‑driven vascular leakage is recognized as a key pathogenic mechanism.
analysis.gaps: Significant gaps remain, including the absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited antiviral efficacy, difficulties in species‑specific diagnosis, and uncertainties driven by ecological changes.
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on vaccine development, antiviral trials, improved diagnostics, and integrated ecological surveillance to mitigate the global hantavirus burden.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses cause HFRS and HPS worldwide, warranting review. Main directions: transmission, virology, immune pathogenesis, limited therapies, diagnostic issues, and environmental drivers are covered. Current state: severity depends on virus type, host immunity, and timely care; immune‑mediated leakage is central. Gaps: no licensed vaccines, modest antivirals, diagnostic limits, and ecological uncertainties persist. Future research: prioritize vaccines, antivirals, better diagnostics, and ecological surveillance.

title: Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak

analysis.background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal.
analysis.methods: This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition‑era travel medicine, drawing on outbreak reports and existing guidance.
analysis.results: The outbreak exposed expedition‑specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post‑disembarkation monitoring; it also led to the proposal of an accountability‑oriented One Health preparedness model.
analysis.contribution: The paper highlights the need for binding, auditable, expedition‑specific outbreak protocols and operationalizes existing guidance through route‑level risk assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, and cross‑border post‑travel monitoring.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.

analysis.background: ANDV uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising concern about its high case fatality and the need for post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
analysis.main_directions: The viewpoint evaluates ribavirin as PEP after high‑risk household, healthcare, or laboratory exposure and presents dosing strategies derived from modelling and simulation.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin exhibits antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, yet human evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited and toxicity concerns restrict its use to selected scenarios.
analysis.gaps: Controlled human studies are absent, optimal dose and duration are undefined, and safety data for ribavirin PEP in ANDV exposure remain insufficient.
analysis.future_research: Conduct controlled clinical trials to determine ribavirin PEP efficacy, optimal dosing, and safety, supported by pharmacokinetic modelling and systematic surveillance of exposed individuals.

analysis.summary_en: Background: ANDV uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising concern about its high case fatality and the need for post‑exposure prophylaxis. Main directions: The viewpoint evaluates ribavirin as PEP after high‑risk household, healthcare, or laboratory exposure and presents dosing strategies derived from modelling. Current state: Ribavirin shows antiviral activity in vitro and animal models, but human evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited and toxicity concerns restrict use. Gaps: No controlled human studies, undefined optimal dose/duration, and limited safety data. Future research: Conduct controlled trials to define efficacy, optimal dosing, and safety, with modelling and surveillance of exposed cohorts.

title: Modeling the Transmission Dynamics of Hantavirus with Environmental Viral Persistence

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure occurring mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles (A1).
analysis.methods: The authors formulate and analyse a deterministic compartmental model that includes susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir (A2).
analysis.results: The model incorporates logistic rodent growth, direct rodent‑to‑rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding and natural clearance (A3); the disease‑free equilibrium and basic reproduction number (R0) are derived (A5); stability analysis shows the disease‑free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1 and an endemic equilibrium exists when R0 > 1 (A6‑A7); environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway sustaining transmission (A8); reducing rodent infection, limiting environmental contamination and increasing viral clearance are suggested to move the system below the epidemic threshold (A9).
analysis.contribution: The study offers a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions involving seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data‑driven parameterisation (A10).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided, so no empirical data, parameter estimation, or model validation are reported (abstract_only).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure via contaminated environments (A1). Methods: A deterministic compartmental model with rodent, human and environmental compartments was formulated and analysed (A2). Results: The model includes logistic rodent growth, direct and indirect transmission, shedding and clearance; the disease‑free equilibrium and R0 were derived, showing stability when R0<1 and endemic persistence when R0>1; environmental persistence sustains transmission, and reducing rodent infection, contamination, or increasing clearance may lower epidemic risk (A3‑A9). Contribution: Provides a tractable framework for understanding hantavirus persistence and guiding future model extensions (A10). Limitations: Only an abstract is available, lacking empirical data, parameterisation, or validation (abstract_only).

title: Hantavirus and Middle East Public Health Challenges: A Subject Review

title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate

analysis.background: Anthropogenic climate change is reshuffling global biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover of rodent‑borne orthohantaviruses, a major public‑health concern.
analysis.methods: The authors used an integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework that combined 89 virus‑host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic force‑of‑infection (FOI) modeling to project spillover hazards under SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5 climate scenarios for 2041–2060.
analysis.results: Models predict a spatially uneven but extensive expansion of spillover risk, with approximately 74.9 % of modeled land area showing increased FOI, and three high‑hazard belts identified in temperate regions of western/central North America, central/eastern Europe into western Russia, and central South America.
analysis.contribution: The resulting global hazard maps provide a baseline for targeted surveillance and underscore the need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public‑health preparedness.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence

analysis.summary_en: Background: Anthropogenic climate change reshuffles biodiversity and accelerates zoonotic spillover of rodent‑borne hantaviruses. Methods: An integrated ecological and machine‑learning framework combined 89 virus‑host links across 61 reservoirs with force‑of‑infection modeling to project hazards under SSP2‑4.5 and SSP5‑8.5 scenarios for 2041‑2060. Results: Approximately 74.9% of global land is projected to experience increased FOI, with three high‑hazard belts in temperate North America, Europe‑Russia, and central South America. Contribution: The hazard maps offer a baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight integrating conservation planning with public‑health preparedness. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

title: First nationwide full-genome characterisation of human-derived Andes virus in Chile: a retrospective genomic epidemiology study.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina.
analysis.methods: Retrospective genomic epidemiology study of ANDV infections in Chile; 58 human clinical samples collected March 9 2011–June 27 2024 were sequenced for the S, M, and L segments; consensus sequences were generated and genetic clustering and divergence assessed using phylogenetic inference and variant calling.
analysis.results: Two major genomic variants were identified with distinct geographical distributions: ANDV Chi‑North (central Chile) and ANDV‑South (southern Chile); no consistent clustering by clinical severity or recurrent non‑synonymous substitutions linked to severe disease; substitutions associated with person‑to‑person transmission in Argentina were not consistently observed, including in four such cases; the Chi‑North lineage remained phylogenetically distinct and geographically restricted.
analysis.contribution: First nationwide genomic characterisation of human‑derived ANDV in Chile; demonstrates that regional diversification drives viral diversity rather than clinical outcome; absence of consistent amino‑acid signatures suggests phenotypes are unlikely explained by viral genetics alone; findings refine understanding of ANDV evolution and underscore the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance.
analysis.limitations: Only abstract available; sample size limited to 58 individuals; detailed clinical data and causal inference not reported in the supplied evidence; specific geographic coordinates and host species beyond humans not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) is the only hantavirus known to transmit between humans and causes hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in Chile and Argentina. Methods: Retrospective genomic epidemiology study of ANDV infections in Chile; 58 human clinical samples collected March 9 2011–June 27 2024 were sequenced for the S, M, and L segments; consensus sequences were generated and genetic clustering and divergence assessed using phylogenetic inference and variant calling. Results: Two major genomic variants were identified with distinct geographical distributions: ANDV Chi‑North (central Chile) and ANDV‑South (southern Chile); no consistent clustering by clinical severity or recurrent non‑synonymous substitutions linked to severe disease; substitutions associated with person‑to‑person transmission in Argentina were not consistently observed, including in four such cases; the Chi‑North lineage remained phylogenetically distinct and geographically restricted. Contribution: First nationwide genomic characterisation of human‑derived ANDV in Chile; demonstrates that regional diversification drives viral diversity rather than clinical outcome; absence of consistent amino‑acid signatures suggests phenotypes are unlikely explained by viral genetics alone; findings refine understanding of ANDV evolution and underscore the need for continued integrated genomic surveillance. title: Bleeding in haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome: A systematic review characterising the loss of haemostasis in hantavirus infections.

analysis.background: HFRS, caused by hantaviruses, presents with variable thrombocytopenia, coagulation abnormalities, and bleeding, yet no prior systematic review has synthesized haemostatic dysfunction evidence.
analysis.main_directions: The review collates data on thrombocytopenia severity across hantavirus species, reports mild prolongation of aPTT and elevated D‑dimer, and highlights limited paediatric observations.
analysis.current_state: Thrombocytopenia is the most consistent abnormality, especially severe in HTNV, DOBV, and SEOV versus milder PUUV; coagulation test changes are reported inconsistently.
analysis.gaps: Study quality and reporting are heterogeneous, preventing meta‑analysis and limiting definitive conclusions.
analysis.future_research: Standardised, prospective investigations of coagulation and bleeding outcomes across hantavirus types and age groups are urgently needed.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HFRS caused by hantaviruses shows variable thrombocytopenia and bleeding, but lacks a systematic synthesis. Main directions: The review details thrombocytopenia severity by virus, mild aPTT/D‑dimer changes, and sparse paediatric data. Current state: Thrombocytopenia is the consistent finding, especially in HTNV, DOBV, SEOV, while coagulation abnormalities vary. Gaps: Heterogeneous study quality and reporting preclude meta‑analysis and firm conclusions. Future research: Calls for standardized, prospective studies of haemostatic parameters and bleeding across hantavirus species and ages.

title: Spatiotemporal correlation analysis between climate change and hantavirus outbreak in Argentina: Quantitative causal inference based on Google Earth Engine and Bayesian spatiotemporal regression

analysis.background: The study addresses a surge in Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) cases in Argentina, with 101 confirmed cases in the 2025–2026 season and a geographic shift of the epidemic epicenter from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region.
analysis.methods: Confirmed HPS cases from 2020–2026 were geocoded and temporally mapped; climate data (ERA5-Land and NOAA GSOD) were accessed via Google Earth Engine to compute temperature anomalies, SPI, SMA, and extreme weather frequencies; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression with INLA estimated lag effects and spatial heterogeneity; causal mediation analysis linked climate anomalies to NDVI, rodent expansion, and human exposure.
analysis.results: Spatial clustering of HPS was significant (Moran's I = 0.42, p < 0.001); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI = 1.31–2.14) with a 3–4 month lag and TA (RR = 1.45 per 1°C, 95% CrI = 1.12–1.87) positively influenced HPS onset, with a synergistic interaction (RERI = 0.79); Buenos Aires risk rose 2.3‑fold after extreme rainfall, NOA outbreaks linked to drought‑flood events; NDVI mediated 41.2% and 56.7% of the climate‑HPS relationship.
analysis.contribution: This is the first national‑scale quantitative causal inference of climate anomalies driving hantavirus outbreaks in Argentina, confirming a drought‑flood–rodent–human exposure pathway and providing a methodological basis for a climate‑driven early warning system.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: HPS cases surged to 101 in 2025–2026, with the epidemic epicenter shifting from Patagonia to Buenos Aires and the NOA region. Methods: HPS cases (2020–2026) were geocoded; climate data from ERA5‑Land and NOAA GSOD were processed via Google Earth Engine to compute temperature anomalies, SPI, SMA, and extreme weather events; Bayesian spatiotemporal Poisson regression with INLA estimated lag effects; causal mediation analysis linked climate anomalies to NDVI, rodent expansion, and human exposure. Results: Spatial clustering was significant (Moran's I = 0.42); SPI (RR = 1.68, 95% CrI = 1.31–2.14) with a 3–4 month lag and TA (RR = 1.45 per 1°C, 95% CrI = 1.12–1.87) increased HPS risk, with a synergistic interaction (RERI = 0.79); Buenos Aires risk rose 2.3‑fold after extreme rainfall, NOA outbreaks linked to drought‑flood events; NDVI mediated 41.2% and 56.7% of the climate‑HPS link. Contribution: First national‑scale causal inference of climate anomalies driving hantavirus outbreaks, confirming a drought‑flood–rodent–human exposure pathway and supporting a climate‑driven early warning system. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.
